docs.title: CQC League Governance Wiki
docs.text:
This wiki captures research on Care Quality Commission (CQC) governance requirements and how they may apply to professional Rugby League club medical services in England.

The core conclusion is practical: most match-day and training-ground treatment for players is likely to sit inside CQC's sports-ground or temporary sporting-event exclusions for treatment of disease, disorder or injury, but a club can still create CQC exposure if it operates a broader private healthcare service, diagnostics pathway, clinic, remote triage service, or subcontracted service where the club controls regulated healthcare activity.

Scope

This is a research and governance design aid, not legal advice. Registration decisions should be checked against the latest CQC guidance and, for borderline service models, with specialist regulatory counsel or CQC.

docs.title: How to use this wiki
docs.text:
• Use Board Briefing as the initial board-facing summary from the Head of Medical.
  • Use Board CQC Response and Outcomes for assessment response, factual accuracy, positive and negative findings, and enforcement planning.
  • Start with CQC Governance Baseline for the regulatory architecture.
  • Read CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements for the assessment lens CQC uses to judge quality.
  • Use CQC Registration Edge Cases for diagnostics, injections, remote advice, ambulance transport, contractors, and public clinic services.
  • Use Professional Rugby League Application to map sports-club medical provision to likely CQC triggers and exclusions.
  • Use Worked Examples to test realistic club service models against the registration and assurance logic.
  • Use RFL Medical Standards Map to connect the 2026 RFL Medical Standards to CQC-style assurance.
  • Use Private Healthcare Synergies to borrow governance controls from independent healthcare providers.
  • Use Medicines, Prescribing, and Anti-Doping for the highest-risk operational interface between clinical care and elite sport.
  • Use Registration Decision Model as the working checklist before launching or changing a medical service.
  • Use CQC Scope Decision Research and CQC Scope Question Bank to identify the facts and evidence needed before any decision tool is designed.
  • Use Governance Operating Model as a board-to-pitch operating template.
  • Use Minimum Compliance Pack Research and Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix to understand the evidence pack before turning it into final templates or software fields.
  • Use Assurance Pack Templates to turn the research into repeatable governance documents.
  • Use Product Research Roadmap, Platform Opportunity, Tool Landscape, Wiki Visual Tooling Register, Product Blueprint, and MVP and Guardrails to shape an online product that combines CQC readiness, sports medical records, evidence management, visual explanation, and governance tooling.
  • Use Research Scope and Assumptions before treating item 1 as product users or buying groups.
  • Use Scope Decision Record for the current working audience and output: an online CQC inspection preparation platform for professional sports clubs, using a Championship Rugby League Club as the starting example.
  • Use Research Validation Plan before moving from research into solution design.

docs.title: Site structure
docs.text:
The research is split into logical sections:

  • Board and Decisions: board briefing, CQC response, registration decision workflow, CQC scope research, question bank, and worked examples.
  • CQC Framework: regulatory baseline, assessment model, edge cases, and private healthcare synergies.
  • Rugby League Application: sport-specific application, RFL standards overview, clause evidence map, clause status register, medicines, prescribing, and anti-doping.
  • Operating Model and Evidence: governance routines, minimum compliance pack research, templates, and evidence packs.
  • Platform Product Research: product roadmap, research scope, scope decision, validation plan, opportunity, tool landscape, visual tooling, tool comparison, product modules, MVP, and guardrails.

docs.title: Assurance Pack Templates
docs.text: These templates turn the research into evidence a club can use repeatedly. They are intentionally concise so they can be copied into board papers, committee packs, contracts, or internal forms. Use them alongside Minimum Compliance Pack Research, CQC's scope of registration guidance, the RFL Medical Standards 2026, and the detailed decision logic in Registration Decision Model.

docs.title: 1. CQC scope assessment
docs.text:
Field Entry Service name Legal entity proposing service Service owner Clinical governance lead Locations Stadium; training ground; academy site; mobile; remote; third-party premises. User group Contracted players; academy; staff; officials; public; sponsors; schools; other. Activities Treatment; diagnostics; screening; prescribing; injections; remote triage; transport; rehabilitation; mental health; other. Regulated activity candidates Treatment of disease, disorder or injury; diagnostic and screening; transport/triage/remote advice; surgical procedures; other. Sports/event exclusion analysis Is care solely for participants or attendees at sports ground, gym, associated premises, or temporary event? Evidence? Provider/control analysis Who controls clinical policy, staff, records, complaints, incidents, medicines, equipment, referrals, and quality assurance? Contractor/host analysis Registered provider? Exemption rationale? Contract responsibility? Audit rights? Location analysis Where is the activity delivered, organised, or managed from? Registered manager needed? Controlled drugs licence issue? Conclusion Outside scope; excluded; contractor registered; club must register; legal advice needed. Reviewer and date Next review trigger New users; public access; diagnostics; prescribing; injections; remote advice; new premises; new contractor.

This template is intended for the edge cases identified in CQC Registration Edge Cases, especially public clinics, diagnostics, remote triage, prescribing, injections, IV services, and contractor-controlled care.

For platform design, this template should become a structured workflow rather than a static document. See Platform Product Blueprint.

docs.title: 2. Medical service statement of purpose
docs.text:
Section Minimum content Service purpose What the club medical service exists to do. Legal entity Club company/charity/partnership and address. Aims and objectives Player welfare, safe participation, emergency care, rehabilitation, compliance with RFL standards. People served First team, reserves, academy, women's teams, scholarship, match officials, staff, public exclusions. Services not provided Public clinic, paid private treatment, remote urgent triage, IV wellness, diagnostics, or other exclusions unless approved. Records and confidentiality System, access, retention, player consent, coach information boundaries. Contractors External providers, registration/exemption evidence, audit and reporting. Review Annual board approval and update after service change.

This mirrors CQC's statement of purpose discipline even where the club concludes it is outside registration.

docs.title: 9. Annual medical governance review
docs.text:
Agenda:

  1. Current CQC registration/exclusion assessment.
  2. RFL Medical Standards compliance.
  3. Clinical governance structure and named leads.
  4. Incidents, complaints, safeguarding, and serious injury reports.
  5. Concussion and return-to-play audit.
  6. Medicines, prescribing, drugs box, anti-doping, and TUE audit.
  7. Cardiac screening and diagnostics provider assurance.
  8. Records, confidentiality, data sharing, and loan-player handovers.
  9. Contractor due diligence and quality reports.
  10. Player experience and clinician speaking-up feedback.
  11. Facilities, equipment, emergency action plans, and infection control.
  12. Risk register and action plan for next season.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This page is a board-facing briefing from the Head of Medical. It explains what the Care Quality Commission (CQC) is, how CQC assessment works, how it may apply to a professional Rugby League club such as Sheffield Eagles in the Betfred Championship, what evidence the club should hold, and how the club should respond to positive or negative CQC assessment outcomes.

This is not a conclusion that Sheffield Eagles, or any other Championship club, is currently CQC-registerable. It is a governance briefing to help the board understand the boundary between sports medical provision and regulated private healthcare.

docs.title: Executive summary for the board
docs.text:
CQC is the independent regulator of health and adult social care in England. It registers providers carrying on regulated activities, assesses quality and safety, publishes ratings and reports for many services, and can take enforcement action where legal requirements are breached.

For a Rugby League club, CQC is most relevant where the club medical service moves beyond participant-only sport care into private healthcare, diagnostics, remote triage, prescribing/injections, public clinic services, or contractor-controlled regulated activity. Core match-day and training-ground care for players can sit within CQC's sports or first-aid exclusions for Treatment of disease, disorder or injury, but that exclusion should be evidenced, not assumed.

The board should direct the Head of Medical to maintain:

  • a documented CQC scope assessment for every medical service;
  • a medical service statement of purpose;
  • a CQC-style five-domain evidence pack: Safe, Effective, Caring, Responsive, Well-led;
  • a contractor due diligence file for external medical, diagnostics, ambulance, and screening providers;
  • a response plan for CQC contact, draft reports, factual accuracy review, enforcement, and public communications.

docs.title: What CQC is
docs.text:
CQC's functions relevant to a club are:

  • Registration: deciding whether the legal entity carrying on a regulated activity can provide that activity lawfully.
  • Assessment: judging quality and safety through the five key questions and quality statements in the CQC assessment framework.
  • Ratings and reports: for many services, publishing ratings from Outstanding, Good, Requires improvement, and Inadequate. CQC's levels of ratings guidance says independent doctors and clinics are rated at service level for each key question and with an aggregated overall service rating.
  • Enforcement: taking proportionate action where registered providers or managers breach regulations.

The board should treat CQC as both a legal registration risk and a useful benchmark for clinical governance, even where the club concludes its core Rugby League medical service is outside registration.

docs.title: How CQC assessments are conducted
docs.text:
CQC's current assessment approach retains the five key questions: Safe, Effective, Caring, Responsive, and Well-led. Under each key question sit quality statements. CQC gathers evidence across six categories: people's experience, staff and leader feedback, partner feedback, observation, processes, and outcomes.

CQC can gather evidence on site and off site. CQC's assessing-quality guidance explains that assessments may be planned or responsive, and that CQC can expand the scope where needed. It can ask providers for evidence, interview staff and leaders, speak with people who use services, observe care and environments, and review records.

For a club, that means an assessment-style review would not be satisfied by policies alone. The club needs working evidence:

  • records showing care was actually documented;
  • incident logs showing learning and closure;
  • equipment checks showing match-day readiness;
  • player feedback showing experience and confidentiality;
  • contractor reports showing external providers are controlled;
  • board minutes showing medical risk is visible and acted on.

See CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements for the full framework.

docs.title: How this might apply to Rugby League
Core player care
docs.text:
For a Championship club, core medical provision usually includes match-day cover, training-ground treatment, rehabilitation, concussion management, emergency action plans, mandatory equipment, and return-to-play controls. For example, the RFL Medical Standards 2026 require medical staff, note keeping, emergency action planning, mandatory medical equipment, anti-doping controls, concussion management, and cardiac screening obligations.

That core player/participant care may fall within the sports-ground, gymnasium, first-aid, or temporary sporting-event exclusions in CQC's treatment guidance. The club still needs a written rationale and a governance pack because CQC, insurers, RFL, legal advisers, or the board may ask how that conclusion was reached.

docs.title: Does the pitch-side sports exclusion apply to professional sport?
docs.text:
Yes, it can. CQC's current treatment guidance excludes treatment provided in a sports ground or gymnasium, including associated premises, where it is provided for the sole benefit of people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events. It also excludes treatment provided through temporary arrangements for sporting or cultural events. The wording does not say that professional sport is excluded from the exclusion.

The professional status of the competition is therefore not the decisive test. The decisive questions are:

  • is the care treatment of disease, disorder or injury?
  • is it provided at a sports ground, gymnasium, associated premises, or temporary sporting event?
  • is it solely for people taking part in or attending the sporting activity or event?
  • is the service only pitch-side/event/player care, or has it become a broader clinic, diagnostic, remote triage, ambulance, or private healthcare service?
  • who is directing and controlling the care?

For a professional club, the practical board position should be: the pitch-side sports exclusion can apply to professional Rugby League, but only within its conditions and only for the relevant regulated activity. The club should not treat it as a blanket exemption for all medical services.

docs.title: Sheffield Eagles / Championship operating context
docs.text:
Using Sheffield Eagles as an illustrative Championship club:

  • the club operates in a professional Rugby League environment with RFL medical standards and Championship fixture obligations;
  • the club announced in April 2026 that it would relocate to The MEPS International Home of Football Stadium from its Betfred Championship Round 14 fixture against London Broncos on 24 May 2026, which makes venue-specific emergency action planning and treatment-room assurance especially important;
  • if the club uses shared premises, temporary arrangements, contractors, or external medical facilities, it should be clear which entity controls medical rooms, equipment, emergency access, records, and provider responsibilities.

The Sheffield-specific governance issue is not "CQC applies because the club is Sheffield Eagles." The issue is whether any medical service model at or around the club crosses a CQC regulated-activity boundary.

docs.title: Triggers that need board approval before launch
docs.text:
The board should require formal approval before the club:

  • opens a public sports injury clinic;
  • treats sponsors, staff families, schools, or community users outside participant/event care;
  • offers paid physiotherapy or sports medicine services to non-players;
  • runs diagnostic ultrasound, cardiac screening, blood testing, imaging, or remote reporting under club control;
  • offers remote injury triage or medical advice beyond normal player follow-up;
  • stores or administers controlled drugs or high-risk medicines;
  • offers injections, IV products, wellness infusions, or recovery treatments;
  • changes event medical, ambulance, diagnostics, or clinic contractors.

Use Worked Examples and CQC Registration Edge Cases for practical scenarios.

docs.title: Evidence required to demonstrate CQC-style compliance
docs.text:
The board should ask for an evidence pack organised by the five CQC domains.

Domain Evidence the board should expect Safe Emergency action plans, mandatory equipment checks, medicines log, infection-control audit, safeguarding records, concussion incidents, staffing rota, adverse event reviews. Effective Clinical protocols, RFL compliance, referral pathways, return-to-play outcomes, cardiac screening completion, rehabilitation audit, clinician CPD and scope of practice. Caring Player consent, confidentiality boundaries, chaperone policy, player feedback, dignity/privacy controls, evidence that clinical judgement is protected from selection pressure. Responsive Complaints and concerns log, academy/parent communication, loan-player handovers, accessible information, rapid escalation pathways, external provider response times. Well-led Board medical risk register, clinical governance committee minutes, contractor due diligence, annual CQC scope review, audit action tracker, named accountable executive and clinical governance lead.

Minimum document set:

  • CQC scope assessment for each service;
  • medical service statement of purpose;
  • legal entity and provider/control analysis;
  • RFL Medical Standards compliance dashboard;
  • clinical policies and SOPs;
  • clinician credential and indemnity register;
  • incident, complaint, safeguarding, and duty-of-candour process;
  • medicines and anti-doping log;
  • contractor due diligence file;
  • board assurance report.

The practical templates are in Assurance Pack Templates.

docs.title: Response and output pack
docs.text:
The board should agree in advance how the club will respond to CQC contact, draft reports, factual accuracy requests, positive outcomes, negative findings, and enforcement risks. Those workflows are now separated into Board CQC Response and Outcomes.

The immediate output pack should include a board paper, CQC scope register, evidence dashboard, medical risk extract, action plan, and appendix covering RFL Medical Standards, contractors, medicines, anti-doping, and concussion assurance.

docs.title: Board CQC Response and Outcomes
docs.text: This page expands the response material from the Board Briefing. It is intended for the Head of Medical, accountable executive, board medical lead, and any club group handling CQC contact, factual accuracy, remediation, or enforcement risk.

docs.title: How to respond to CQC assessment or contact
Before any CQC contact
docs.text:
The club should already have:

  • one accountable executive for CQC/regulatory matters;
  • one clinical lead who owns evidence quality;
  • a single evidence index and document store;
  • up-to-date registered details if any registered service exists;
  • legal advice route for registration and enforcement questions;
  • communications protocol for players, staff, media, and partners.

docs.title: During an assessment
docs.text:
If CQC contacts the club or a registered service:

  • establish exactly which legal entity, location, regulated activity, and service model CQC is asking about;
  • appoint a single response lead and evidence coordinator;
  • preserve clinical records, incident logs, contracts, audit records, and board minutes;
  • answer honestly and precisely, distinguishing player-only sports care from public/private healthcare;
  • keep a log of requests, documents supplied, meetings, interviews, and actions promised;
  • escalate any immediate safety issue to the board and clinical governance lead the same day.

docs.title: Draft report and factual accuracy
docs.text:
CQC sends draft reports through its factual accuracy process for many assessments. The registered manager, nominated individual, or main partner normally accesses the factual accuracy portal.

Board direction:

  • treat factual accuracy as evidence correction, not public relations;
  • challenge only points that are factually wrong, incomplete in a materially misleading way, or unsupported by evidence;
  • attach supporting documents where relevant;
  • keep comments concise and tied to the quality statement or evidence category;
  • approve any challenge with legal and clinical review before submission.

docs.title: Positive outcome
docs.text:
A positive assessment, or a decision that a service is outside CQC registration, should not be treated as "done." The club should:

  • report the outcome to the board;
  • preserve the evidence pack and rationale;
  • update the statement of purpose and risk register;
  • communicate appropriately to players, staff, insurers, and partners;
  • schedule annual review and service-change triggers;
  • use the result to strengthen RFL, insurer, and stakeholder confidence.

docs.title: Requires improvement or negative findings
docs.text:
If CQC identifies concerns, the board should respond with visible control:

  • appoint an executive owner for the remediation plan;
  • address immediate safety issues first;
  • write a corrective action plan with owners, dates, evidence, and board reporting;
  • increase clinical audit frequency;
  • review staffing, records, medicines, safeguarding, complaints, and contractor controls;
  • consider whether the service should pause, narrow scope, or stop accepting public/private patients until safe;
  • prepare communications that are accurate, transparent, and consistent with legal advice.

docs.title: Enforcement
docs.text:
CQC's enforcement guidance says action should be proportionate to impact and seriousness. CQC may issue warning notices, vary or impose conditions, suspend or cancel registration, prosecute in serious cases, or use other regulatory tools.

If CQC issues a Warning Notice, the provider can normally make written representations within 10 working days. CQC can set a compliance timescale and will check whether the provider has complied. For other enforcement action, CQC may issue a notice of proposal, allow representations, and then issue a notice of decision that can be appealed to an independent tribunal.

Board direction:

  • do not treat enforcement as a medical department issue only;
  • form a board sub-group for regulatory response;
  • get legal advice immediately;
  • separate factual representations from remediation action;
  • continue safety improvements even while representations are being considered;
  • maintain a clear communications line with CQC, RFL, insurers, players, and staff.

docs.title: Recommended initial output pack
docs.text:
The Head of Medical should produce the following for the next board cycle:

  1. Board paper: the Board Briefing condensed to 2 to 4 pages.
  2. CQC scope register: every club medical service, user group, location, and CQC conclusion.
  3. Evidence dashboard: Safe, Effective, Caring, Responsive, Well-led.
  4. Risk register extract: top five medical regulatory risks.
  5. Action plan: gaps, owners, deadlines, board decisions needed.
  6. Appendix: RFL Medical Standards compliance, contractor due diligence, medicines/anti-doping controls, concussion assurance.

If the club wants to turn this into a repeatable digital process rather than a spreadsheet/document pack, see Platform Opportunity.

docs.title: Immediate board decisions
docs.text:
The board should decide:

  • whether the club will operate a strict player/participant-only medical service or allow wider private healthcare activity;
  • whether any current or planned public, diagnostic, prescribing, injection, remote triage, or contractor service needs legal/CQC review;
  • who is the accountable executive for medical governance;
  • what quarterly board reporting format will be used;
  • whether the club should commission an independent CQC scope review.

docs.title: CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements
Provider assurance framework
docs.text:
For registered providers, CQC's current assessment framework retains the five key questions. These are the practical domains of assurance:

  1. Safe: people are protected from avoidable harm, abuse, neglect, discrimination, unsafe environments, unsafe staffing, infection risks, and medicines risks.
  2. Effective: care and treatment are evidence based, coordinated, consented to, outcome focused, and based on assessed needs.
  3. Caring: people are treated with kindness, compassion, dignity, choice, control, and respect for individual needs.
  4. Responsive: services are organised around people, accessible, continuous, inclusive, and able to respond to feedback and complaints.
  5. Well-led: leadership, culture, governance, risk management, partnership working, learning, and sustainability support safe and high-quality care.

CQC's 34 quality statements are written as "we" statements. They describe the commitments providers, commissioners, and system leaders are expected to live up to. They replaced the older key lines of enquiry.

docs.title: The 34 provider quality statements
docs.text: Domain Quality statements Safe Learning culture; Safe systems, pathways and transitions; Safeguarding; Involving people to manage risks; Safe environments; Safe and effective staffing; Infection prevention and control; Medicines optimisation. Effective Assessing needs; Delivering evidence-based care and treatment; How staff, teams and services work together; Supporting people to live healthier lives; Monitoring and improving outcomes; Consent to care and treatment. Caring Kindness, compassion and dignity; Treating people as individuals; Independence, choice and control; Responding to people's immediate needs; Workforce wellbeing and enablement. Responsive Person-centred care; Care provision, integration and continuity; Providing information; Listening to and involving people; Equity in access; Equity in experiences and outcomes; Planning for the future. Well-led Shared direction and culture; Capable, compassionate and inclusive leaders; Freedom to speak up; Workforce equality, diversity and inclusion; Governance, management and sustainability; Partnerships and communities; Learning, improvement and innovation; Environmental sustainability and sustainable development.

docs.title: Evidence categories
docs.text:
CQC groups assessment evidence into six evidence categories:

  • people's experience of health and care services;
  • feedback from staff and leaders;
  • feedback from partners;
  • observation;
  • processes;
  • outcomes.

For a club medical service, these categories are useful even where the activity is outside CQC registration because they convert governance into inspectable evidence. For example, a concussion protocol is a process; player feedback is people's experience; RFL, ambulance, hospital, or contractor feedback is partner evidence; medical-room checks are observation/process evidence; return-to-play and reinjury rates are outcomes. The RFL Medical Standards Map shows how RFL evidence can be organised under these domains.

docs.title: Rugby League medical evidence map
docs.text: CQC domain What assurance should look like in a professional Rugby League club Safe Match-day and training emergency action plans; safeguarding escalation; concussion and return-to-play controls; medical-room risk assessments; emergency equipment checks; infection prevention; medicines storage, prescribing, injections, and adverse-event review; sufficient IMMOFP/iIMMOFP and allied health cover. Effective Clinical assessment standards; evidence-based injury management; referral pathways to imaging, hospitals, specialists, and rehabilitation; multidisciplinary reviews; consent for treatment and information sharing; outcome tracking for return to play, reinjury, and clinical recovery. Caring Player dignity, privacy, informed choices, confidentiality, chaperone use, psychological safety, and respectful handling of selection-pressure conflicts; staff wellbeing for clinicians working in high-pressure sport environments. Responsive Accessible complaint and concern routes; timely treatment after injury; accessible information for players and academy families; adaptation for disability, language, culture, neurodiversity, and age; joined-up care across club, governing body, NHS, private providers, and away fixtures. Well-led Board ownership of medical risk; independent clinical authority; clinical governance committee; risk register; incident learning; credentialing; contractor assurance; Freedom to Speak Up route; equality and inclusion; audit; sustainability of medical staffing and facilities.

docs.title: Priority quality statements for club governance
docs.text:
A club medical governance model should give special attention to these statements:

Quality statement Why it matters for a Rugby League club Learning culture Serious injuries, near misses, concussion events, failed processes, and emergency transfers need logged learning and closed actions. Safe systems, pathways and transitions Players move between pitch-side care, club medical rooms, ambulance services, emergency departments, imaging, specialists, surgery, rehabilitation, and return to play. Safeguarding Academy players, young professionals, vulnerable adults, and power imbalances require explicit safeguarding controls. Safe environments Stadium and training-ground medical rooms, equipment, technology, cleaning, storage, and emergency access need regular assurance. Safe and effective staffing Club cover depends on qualified, competent, indemnified, and supervised doctors, physiotherapists, therapists, rehabilitators, paramedics, and first aiders. Medicines optimisation Pain relief, injections, prescribing, emergency medicines, anti-doping implications, and storage controls are high-risk in elite sport. Delivering evidence-based care and treatment Clinical decisions should be based on current medical evidence and sport-specific standards, not selection pressure. Monitoring and improving outcomes Injury recurrence, return-to-play outcomes, concussion metrics, complaints, and player experience should drive improvement. Consent to care and treatment Consent must cover treatment, examination, referrals, information sharing with coaches, and the limits of confidentiality. Listening to and involving people Players need safe routes to raise concerns about care, pressure to play, confidentiality, and welfare. Equity in access, experiences and outcomes Academy, women's, disability, reserve, overseas, neurodivergent, and injured players should not receive weaker medical access or outcomes. Governance, management and sustainability This is the central board assurance statement: roles, accountability, risk, performance, records, audits, and sustainability. Freedom to speak up Players and clinicians need a route to escalate safety concerns without fear of selection, contract, or employment consequences. Partnerships and communities The club must work well with RFL, hospitals, ambulance services, private clinics, insurers, schools, and safeguarding agencies.

docs.title: Local authority assurance is different
docs.text:
CQC also has a separate local authority assessment framework for duties under Part 1 of the Care Act 2014. That framework uses four themes and nine quality statements. It is not the main framework for a professional sports club or private healthcare provider, but it is relevant terminology because CQC calls it "assurance."

The four local authority assurance themes are:

  1. Working with people.
  2. Providing support.
  3. How the local authority ensures safety within the system.
  4. Leadership.

When discussing club medical services, avoid importing the local authority framework unless the issue is partnership with council adult social care or safeguarding systems. For club/private healthcare governance, use the five provider domains above.

docs.title: Practical use
docs.text:
Use the five provider domains as the headings for board assurance:

  • Safe: are immediate risks controlled?
  • Effective: does clinical care follow evidence and achieve outcomes?
  • Caring: are players treated as people, not only performance assets?
  • Responsive: does the service adapt, listen, and stay accessible?
  • Well-led: can the board prove the medical system is governed, learning, and sustainable?

docs.title: What CQC regulates
docs.text:
CQC registration is activity-based. Under CQC's scope of registration guidance, a provider may need to register if it provides, or intends to provide, health or adult social care activities in England. CQC states that this can apply even where the service is not regular or permanent, and that carrying on a regulated activity without registration is an offence.

The practical test is:

  1. Is a regulated activity being carried on?
  2. Who is directing and controlling it?
  3. Does an exception or exemption apply?
  4. Where is the activity carried on at or from?
  5. Is a registered manager required?

For professional sports medical provision, the most relevant regulated activity is usually Treatment of disease, disorder or injury. Depending on the service model, other activities may also matter, especially diagnostic and screening procedures, surgical procedures, transport services, remote triage or medical advice, and any treatment delivered from a clinic not covered by a sports-specific exclusion. The practical boundary cases are developed in CQC Registration Edge Cases.

docs.title: Provider, location, and control
docs.text:
CQC registration follows the legal entity that carries on the regulated activity. That may be an individual, partnership, company, charity, voluntary body, NHS body, or other organisation.

For sports clubs this matters because medical provision often involves several parties:

  • the club operating medical rooms and employing or contracting clinicians;
  • an independent sports medicine clinic providing services at club premises;
  • a governing body setting medical standards but not directing individual care;
  • event medical providers covering fixtures;
  • NHS or private hospitals receiving referrals;
  • contracted imaging, blood testing, rehabilitation, or occupational health providers.

CQC expects providers to be clear in contracts and service level agreements about who has responsibility for direction and control of regulated activities. If multiple entities carry on a regulated activity, each may need to consider registration separately. For club outsourcing controls, see Private Healthcare Synergies and the contractor due diligence checklist.

docs.title: Governance requirements
docs.text:
The main governance anchor is Regulation 17: Good governance. CQC guidance expects providers to have systems and processes to assess, monitor, and improve quality and safety; assess, monitor, and mitigate risk; keep accurate records; maintain staff and management records; and use accurate information to run the service.

For a club medical service, Regulation 17 translates into:

  • a named accountable executive or board committee for medical quality and safety;
  • a clinical governance lead with authority independent of sporting performance pressure;
  • a risk register covering player welfare, match-day care, medicines, diagnostics, referrals, infection prevention, safeguarding, data protection, emergency response, and contractor oversight;
  • routine clinical audit, incident review, complaints review, and learning actions;
  • contemporaneous clinical records and secure information governance;
  • evidence that concerns are escalated and acted on without delay.

docs.title: Fundamental standards most relevant to club medicine
docs.text: Regulation Relevance to club medical provision Regulation 9: Person-centred care Care planning, player preferences, equality, and individual needs. Regulation 11: Need for consent Consent for examination, treatment, information sharing, imaging, injections, and return-to-play decisions. Regulation 12: Safe care and treatment Risk assessment, medicines, infection prevention, emergency plans, equipment safety, and clinician competence. Regulation 13: Safeguarding Children, academy players, vulnerable adults, abuse, coercion, and improper treatment. Regulation 15: Premises and equipment Medical rooms, pitch-side equipment, storage, calibration, cleaning, and maintenance. Regulation 16: Complaints Accessible complaint routes for players and others receiving care. Regulation 17: Good governance Quality assurance, audits, records, risk management, and accountability. Regulation 18: Staffing Sufficient suitably qualified, competent, skilled, and experienced staff. Regulation 19: Fit and proper persons employed Recruitment checks, professional registration, indemnity, scope of practice, and ongoing fitness. Regulation 20: Duty of candour Openness after notifiable safety incidents where CQC-regulated care is being provided.

docs.title: CQC assessment lens
docs.text:
CQC's assessment framework asks whether services are safe, effective, caring, responsive, and well-led. These five key questions are the practical assurance domains for registered providers. CQC now assesses them through quality statements, which replace the former key lines of enquiry.

The most directly relevant quality statement for governance is Governance, management and sustainability, which looks for clear responsibilities, accountability, risk and performance information, robust data arrangements, and use of recognised standards. See CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements for the full framework and a sports-club evidence map.

For sports clubs, the evidence bundle should therefore be built around what CQC would recognise:

  • documented responsibilities and escalation routes;
  • clinical policies and protocols;
  • clinician credentials and CPD records;
  • incident, concussion, safeguarding, and complaint logs;
  • audit schedules and action tracking;
  • medicines and equipment governance;
  • player feedback and staff feedback;
  • contractual oversight of independent providers;
  • board reports that show risk, performance, outcomes, and learning.

docs.title: Why this page matters
docs.text:
The largest CQC risk for a professional Rugby League club is not ordinary pitch-side first aid. It is service creep: the medical department gradually becomes a private healthcare provider, diagnostic provider, remote triage service, or controller of outsourced regulated activity.

CQC's current scope of registration is activity-based. The question is not "is this sport?" but:

  1. What regulated activity is being carried on?
  2. Which legal entity directs and controls it?
  3. Is the activity excluded or exempt?
  4. Where is the activity carried on at or from?
  5. Is a registered manager, nominated individual, statement of purpose, location, or controlled-drugs licence needed?

docs.title: Edge-case matrix
docs.text: Scenario CQC registration risk Practical position Pitch-side emergency care during a fixture Lower Usually first-aid/event context. Keep emergency action plan, equipment checks, incident records, and onward transfer records. Training-ground injury assessment for contracted players Lower to medium Likely sports-ground/gym/associated-premises context if solely for participants. Document the exclusion rationale and keep clinical governance controls. Club medical room treats sponsors, staff families, schools, community players, or public paying patients High This looks like private healthcare. Run a formal CQC scope assessment before launch. Diagnostic ultrasound, X-ray, MRI referral pathway, cardiac screening, blood tests sent to a lab Medium to high Diagnostic and screening procedures may apply depending on who performs, analyses, reports, and controls the pathway. Use registered providers or register where required. Physiotherapist-only standalone treatment Often lower for Treatment of disease, disorder or injury Physiotherapists are not listed for that specific regulated activity, but ultrasound by registered physiotherapists has specific diagnostic exemptions and other activities may still be triggered. Club doctor prescribing, administering injections, IV products, or emergency medicines Medium to high Treatment of disease, disorder or injury may apply when prescribing/administering medicines requires a listed healthcare professional, unless the sports exclusion clearly covers the activity. Medicines governance is still required. IV hydration or wellness infusions for athletes High CQC treats IV administration of prescription-only/wellness products as potentially registrable treatment when delivered by or under a listed healthcare professional and claimed to alter physiological state. Anti-doping risk is also high. Remote injury triage hotline or urgent medical advice service Medium to high CQC regulates remote medical advice/triage where immediate action or prioritisation is provided by a body established for that purpose. Occasional informal advice is different. Ambulance or medical vehicle only moves an injured player within the event boundary Lower for transport activity CQC says event-site internal movement is outside the transport regulated activity. Movement from the event ground to hospital can trigger ambulance registration. Contractor provides imaging, event medical cover, or clinic sessions at club premises Medium to high The entity with operational policies, staffing, clinical governance, complaints, and quality assurance may need registration. Contracts must identify control. Club hosts an independent registered sports medicine clinic Medium Hosting alone does not require the host to register, but unclear responsibility can pull the host into accountability. Use written hosting agreements. Academy medical care Medium Sports exclusions may still apply, but safeguarding, consent, records, confidentiality, and parent/guardian communication need stronger controls.

docs.title: Treatment of disease, disorder or injury
docs.text:
This is the main activity to test for club medical departments. CQC's Treatment of disease, disorder or injury guidance says it covers treatment by or under the supervision of a listed healthcare professional or a relevant multidisciplinary team, for a disease, disorder, or injury. It includes emergency treatment, ongoing treatment, physical and mental health treatment, vaccinations, immunisations, and palliative care.

The important sport-specific exclusions are:

  • first aid in unexpected or potentially dangerous situations requiring immediate action;
  • first aid by trained non-healthcare professionals;
  • first aid by organisations established to provide first aid;
  • treatment in a sports ground or gymnasium, including associated premises, solely for people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events;
  • temporary treatment arrangements for sporting or cultural events.

The exclusion should be documented, not assumed. The more a service resembles a public clinic, commercial treatment service, diagnostics business, or routine healthcare pathway, the weaker the sports-exclusion argument becomes.

docs.title: Professional pitch-side care
docs.text:
The sports-ground and temporary sporting-event exclusions can apply to professional sport. CQC's wording is not limited to amateur, volunteer, community, or non-commercial sport. For Rugby League, the right question is not whether the club is professional; it is whether the treatment is:

  • provided in a sports ground, gymnasium, associated premises, or temporary sporting-event arrangement;
  • solely for people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events;
  • confined to the relevant treatment activity rather than a broader healthcare service.

This means a Championship or Super League club can reasonably document an exclusion rationale for pitch-side care, match-day treatment, and participant-only training-ground care. But that rationale does not automatically cover:

  • a public sports injury clinic;
  • diagnostics or screening carried on as a separate activity;
  • remote triage services;
  • ambulance transport from the event ground to hospital;
  • IV wellness or recovery services;
  • treatment of sponsors, schools, community users, or staff families;
  • an external provider whose own regulated activity is not covered by the club's sports context.

For board papers, the safest formulation is: the pitch-side sports exclusion can apply to professional Rugby League, but it is conditional and service-specific.

docs.title: Diagnostics and screening
docs.text:
Diagnostics are a likely expansion point. CQC's diagnostic and screening procedures guidance includes radiation, ultrasound, MRI, most forms of endoscopy, sample removal or examination for diagnostic reasons, and physiological measurement needed to plan or deliver care.

Practical implications for clubs:

  • a club that performs musculoskeletal ultrasound as a club-controlled diagnostic service should assess scope carefully;
  • a registered physiotherapist's use of ultrasound has a specific exemption, but this does not automatically exempt a doctor-led or club-controlled diagnostic service;
  • if a third party reports images remotely, that reporting provider may also need registration;
  • if the club sends athletes to an external registered imaging provider, the club should keep referral and result-governance controls rather than duplicating registration unnecessarily;
  • cardiac screening is a governance obligation under the RFL Medical Standards 2026 for some clubs, and the provider/control model must be clear.

docs.title: Remote advice and triage
docs.text:
CQC's transport, triage and medical advice provided remotely guidance is narrower than ordinary remote communication. It covers medical advice or triage by telephone or email where immediate attention, action, or prioritisation is needed and where the body is established for that purpose.

Club risk increases if the medical department offers:

  • a formal injury triage line;
  • an app-based urgent advice service;
  • remote prescribing;
  • immediate-priority decision making for people beyond the club's own players;
  • advice to the public, schools, sponsors, or partner clubs.

Occasional remote follow-up with a known player may be lower risk, but it still needs records, consent, confidentiality, escalation, and safety-netting.

docs.title: Hosted and subcontracted services
docs.text:
CQC distinguishes hosting from carrying on the regulated activity in its legal entity and provider guidance. A host can make facilities available to another provider, but the provider carrying on the regulated activity must register. If responsibilities are unclear, the host can be exposed because it controls the premises or pathway.

For clubs, contracts should answer:

  • who owns clinical policies and protocols?
  • who recruits, credentials, supervises, and appraises clinicians?
  • whose clinical governance system covers the care?
  • who handles complaints, incidents, safeguarding, records, duty of candour, and notifications?
  • who controls medicines, equipment, diagnostics, and referrals?
  • who pays, invoices, and sets service eligibility?

If the club keeps clinical governance and day-to-day control, it should not rely on the contractor's registration analysis without checking the position.

docs.title: Registration-readiness triggers
docs.text:
Escalate for legal/CQC review before launch if any of these apply:

  • the service treats anyone who is not a player, participant, match official, staff member needing occupational-style advice, or event attendee;
  • the service is advertised externally as a clinic;
  • the club charges fees for medical care;
  • imaging, blood tests, cardiac screening, injections, IV products, minor procedures, or remote triage are added;
  • services are delivered away from the stadium, training ground, gym, or associated premises;
  • the provider/control model is unclear;
  • controlled drugs will be stored or administered;
  • children or academy players are treated outside normal club medical arrangements.

Use the CQC scope assessment template before approving any of these changes.

docs.title: CQC Scope Decision Research
docs.text:
This page records the research needed before any "CQC scope decision wizard" is designed. The goal is to understand the regulatory decision, not to jump directly to software. The question set is captured separately in CQC Scope Question Bank.

The core research question is: what facts must a professional Rugby League club establish before it can safely conclude that a medical service is outside scope, excluded, contractor-led, or potentially registerable?

Use this page with Registration Decision Model, CQC Registration Edge Cases, Minimum Compliance Pack Research, and Worked Examples.

docs.title: Source anchors
docs.text:
CQC's scope guidance identifies the main questions: whether a regulated activity is carried on, who directs and controls it, whether exceptions apply, where it is carried on, and whether registered manager or other registration requirements follow.

For Rugby League, the most important regulated activity is usually treatment of disease, disorder or injury. CQC's current guidance says that this activity can include emergency treatment and treatment by or under supervision of specified healthcare professionals, but it excludes treatment in a sports ground or gymnasium where provided solely for people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events, and temporary arrangements for sporting or cultural events.

Other activities may still matter, especially diagnostic and screening procedures and transport services, triage and medical advice provided remotely.

docs.title: Decision facts to research
docs.text: Decision area Research question Evidence to collect Why it matters Service description What exactly is being provided? Service description, location list, users, clinician roles, activities, payment route, records, referrals, follow-up. CQC registration depends on activity and control, not the label "club medical". User group Is care solely for players/participants/attendees, or is it offered to staff, public, sponsors, schools, or private patients? Eligibility rules, booking forms, communications, invoices, website copy. Public or wider private care may undermine reliance on sports exclusions. Activity Is there treatment of disease, disorder or injury, diagnostics, screening, remote advice, triage, transport, surgery, prescribing, injections, IV treatment, mental health care, or rehabilitation? Clinical pathway, SOPs, medicine lists, diagnostic/screening forms, remote advice logs. Different regulated activities have different tests and exclusions. Professional involvement Is care by, or under supervision of, a listed healthcare professional for the relevant regulated activity? Role register, professional registration, supervision protocol, delegated task record. CQC's treatment activity turns on specified professional involvement or supervision. Sports exclusion Is treatment at a sports ground, gymnasium, associated premises, or temporary event, and solely for those taking part in or attending the event? Venue map, event plan, fixture/training schedule, access rules, attendee/participant evidence. This is the central Rugby League exclusion question. Provider/control Who directs and controls clinical policy, staff, records, medicines, complaints, incidents, quality, and data decisions? Contracts, governance chart, policies, record-system ownership, incident routes, complaint process. A club can create exposure if it controls a registerable activity even where clinicians are contractors. Location Where is the activity carried on at or from? Stadium/training ground details, third-party premises, remote base, mobile unit, away/travel plan. CQC location rules can matter if registration is required. Contractor status If an external provider is used, is it registered, exempt, or out of scope for the actual service? CQC provider profile, regulated activities, location conditions, written exemption analysis, contract. Contractor registration does not automatically cover everything the club does. Change triggers What would invalidate the previous conclusion? Change log, new service proposal, new user group, new premises, new medicine/procedure, new contractor. A scope conclusion must be reviewed when facts change.

docs.title: Provisional outcome types
docs.text:
These are research labels, not legal conclusions.

Outcome label Meaning Minimum evidence Outside CQC scope The service does not appear to involve a CQC regulated activity. Service description, activity analysis, professional involvement analysis, review date. Sports/event exclusion likely applies Treatment appears limited to sports-ground/gym/associated premises or temporary event provision, solely for participants or attendees. Venue/event evidence, user-group rules, activity description, exclusion rationale. Contractor-led An external provider appears to carry on the activity and the club is commissioning/hosting rather than directing clinical provision. Contract, provider registration/exemption evidence, control analysis, quality reporting route. Mixed or borderline Facts are unclear or activities/user groups are mixed. Gap list, risk analysis, pause/ring-fence recommendation, legal/CQC advice record. Potential registration risk Club may carry on a regulated activity without a clear exclusion. Regulated activity analysis, provider/control evidence, locations, registered manager/nominated individual considerations, advice plan.

docs.title: Research tasks for item 5
docs.text: Task Output Convert each edge case into decision facts Update CQC Registration Edge Cases with the factual questions that drive the answer. Build a question bank Create neutral research questions before building any interface. Define evidence required per answer Link each answer to documents, records, screenshots, contracts, or board approvals. Identify "stop and seek advice" points Mark public clinics, diagnostics, remote advice, IV services, prescribing expansion, ambulance transport, and shared-control models. Test against worked examples Check the question set against player-only care, concussion, cardiac screening, academy, public clinic, contractor, and remote triage scenarios. Validate language Ensure the tool says "appears", "likely", and "seek advice" where appropriate, not "CQC compliant".

docs.title: Research-complete definition
docs.text:
Item 5 should only be marked research-complete when:

  • the decision facts above have been tested against the Worked Examples;
  • each possible outcome has minimum supporting evidence;
  • unclear or high-risk fact patterns trigger advice rather than an automated conclusion;
  • contractor and hosted-service questions are explicit;
  • change triggers are documented so the conclusion is not treated as permanent.

docs.title: CQC Scope Question Bank
docs.text:
This page hardens item 5 by converting the CQC Scope Decision Research into a neutral question bank. It is not a decision wizard. It is a research tool for collecting facts before any conclusion is reached.

The language should remain cautious: "appears", "likely", "needs review", and "seek advice" are safer than "CQC compliant".

docs.title: Question bank
docs.text: Question Evidence to collect Stop or seek advice trigger What service is being assessed? Service description, owner, locations, users, activities, clinicians, records, referrals, payment route. The service is not clearly described or has changed since the last assessment. Who is the service for? Eligibility criteria, booking route, website copy, contracts, player/academy/staff/public distinction. Staff families, sponsors, schools, community users, or public patients are included. Is the activity treatment of disease, disorder or injury? Clinical pathway, professional roles, supervision, treatment records, medicines/procedures. Doctor-led treatment, prescribing, injections, IV treatment, or non-player treatment is included. Is the activity diagnostic or screening? Screening plan, ECG/echo/ultrasound/MRI/blood-test pathway, provider reports, referral rules. The club controls testing, analysis, reporting, or clinical follow-up. Is there remote advice, triage, or prioritisation? App/hotline description, response-time promise, triage script, escalation pathway, user group. The body is established to provide urgent advice, triage, or immediate prioritisation. Is there ambulance transport from the event ground to hospital? Contractor service spec, vehicle use, transfer records, CQC provider profile. Contractor or club transports patients to hospital or another healthcare facility. Is the sports-ground/event exclusion being relied on? Venue/event evidence, participant/attendee status, temporary event plan, associated-premises rationale. Service is not solely for participants/attendees or is detached from sports premises/events. Who directs and controls clinical provision? Contracts, governance chart, policies, records ownership, incident route, complaints route, medicines ownership. Club and contractor share control without clear allocation. Who owns records and data-controller decisions? Record-system contract, privacy notices, access matrix, sharing agreement, retention rules. Club has access to full clinical notes without clear role and lawful basis. How are complaints, incidents, serious injuries, safeguarding, and duty-of-candour reviews handled? Policies, reporting routes, serious incident log, contractor escalation, board reports. No clear route exists or registered provider responsibilities are unclear. What locations are used? Stadium, training ground, academy site, third-party clinic, mobile unit, remote administrative base, away venue. Care is delivered from a public clinic, third-party premises, or remote service base. What would trigger reassessment? Change log, board approvals, new service proposals, new providers, new activities, new user groups. Any new diagnostics, public access, remote triage, prescribing/injections/IV, ambulance transport, or contractor change.

docs.title: Scenario test
docs.text: Scenario Questions most likely to drive conclusion Likely evidence burden Player-only match/training medical service user group; sports exclusion; provider/control; records; RFL evidence. Moderate: evidence participant-only scope, premises/event link, governance, RFL controls, and no public clinic drift. External cardiac screening provider diagnostic/screening; contractor status; provider/control; records; abnormal-result pathway. High: provider registration/exemption, contract, clinical control, completion dashboard, and results governance. Public/private clinic or paid treatment user group; treatment; payment route; premises; provider/control; registration route. Very high: stop before launch and seek specialist CQC/legal advice. Remote triage/advice remote advice; user group; triage/prioritisation; records; safeguarding/parental consent. High: distinguish follow-up from formal triage service and document escalation/safety-netting. Concussion and return-to-play sports exclusion; RFL Section E; records; information sharing; medication masking; loan-player handover. Moderate to high clinical governance burden, lower registration risk where event/participant-only facts hold. Contractor-led ambulance/event medical cover contractor status; transport; command/control; incident route; records; event exclusion. High: contract, CQC profile/exemption, command structure, EAP, post-event report, and transport-to-hospital boundary.

docs.title: Minimum outcome evidence
docs.text: Outcome label Questions that must be answered Evidence threshold Outside CQC scope service description; activity; professional involvement. Clear evidence no regulated activity is being carried on. Sports/event exclusion likely applies user group; venue/event; treatment activity; exclusion evidence. Evidence that care is solely for participants/attendees at sports premises/events. Contractor-led provider/control; contractor status; records; incident route. Contract and provider evidence show contractor carries on and governs the relevant activity. Mixed or borderline any unclear question above. Gap list, risk rating, ring-fence/pause recommendation, advice record. Potential registration risk regulated activity; no clear exclusion; club control; location. Full registration/legal advice pack before launch or continuation.

docs.title: Board-to-clinical structure
docs.text:
This operating model translates CQC Regulation 17: Good governance, the well-led governance quality statement, and the RFL Medical Standards 2026 into club routines.

flowchart TB
  Board["Club Board / Ownership Entity"] --> Risk["Board Risk or Medical Governance Committee"]
  Risk --> Exec["Accountable Executive"]
  Exec --> CGLead["Clinical Governance Lead"]
  CGLead --> ClubDoc["Club Doctor / Head of Medical"]
  CGLead --> Safeguard["Safeguarding Lead"]
  CGLead --> IG["Information Governance Lead"]
  CGLead --> Contractors["Independent Providers and Event Medical Contractors"]
  ClubDoc --> MDT["Doctors, Physios, Therapists, Rehabilitators, Paramedics"]
  MDT --> Players["Players / Academy / Participants"]

docs.title: Standing governance forums
docs.text: Forum Frequency Core business Medical governance committee Monthly in season, quarterly out of season Risk register, incidents, complaints, audits, safeguarding, concussion, contractor reports. Match/event medical review Every home fixture or event block Cover, equipment, incidents, transfers, learning actions. Concussion and return-to-play review As cases arise, with periodic audit Protocol compliance, clinical sign-off, recurrence, player communication. Contractor quality review Quarterly or contract-defined Registration/exemption status, credentials, incidents, records, audit results. Board assurance report Quarterly Top risks, compliance status, serious incidents, trends, resources, decisions needed.

docs.title: Core policies
docs.text:
• clinical governance and accountability;
  • scope of service and eligibility;
  • consent and confidentiality;
  • medical records and retention;
  • data protection and information sharing with coaching/performance staff;
  • safeguarding children and adults at risk;
  • chaperones and intimate examinations;
  • concussion, head injury, and return to play;
  • emergency action and ambulance transfer;
  • medicines management, prescribing, injections, and storage;
  • infection prevention and control;
  • equipment maintenance and emergency-kit checks;
  • incident reporting, serious incident review, and learning;
  • complaints and concerns;
  • duty of candour where regulated care is provided;
  • contractor approval and monitoring;
  • clinician credentialing, induction, supervision, and CPD;
  • business continuity and major incident response.

docs.title: Board assurance template
docs.text:
The board should receive a concise dashboard with:

  • a five-domain CQC-style view: safe, effective, caring, responsive, and well-led, using CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements;
  • current CQC scope assessment and any service changes;
  • RFL medical standards compliance;
  • medical risk register top five;
  • staffing and credential compliance;
  • incidents, near misses, concussions, emergency transfers, and themes;
  • complaints, concerns, safeguarding, whistleblowing, and duty-of-candour cases;
  • audit results and overdue actions;
  • contractor registration/exemption status;
  • medicines/equipment exceptions;
  • decisions needed from the board.

docs.title: Five-domain dashboard
docs.text: Domain Suggested board indicators Safe Incidents, near misses, safeguarding, concussion events, emergency transfers, medicines exceptions, equipment and premises checks, staffing gaps. Effective Clinical audit, referral timeliness, return-to-play outcomes, reinjury rates, evidence-based protocol compliance, consent audit. Caring Player feedback, confidentiality concerns, dignity/chaperone audit, clinical independence concerns, clinician wellbeing. Responsive Complaints and concerns, access delays, academy/family information, equality issues, pathway delays with external providers. Well-led Risk register movement, overdue actions, contractor assurance, credential compliance, governance meeting attendance, board decisions and escalations.

docs.title: Red flags
docs.text:
Escalate immediately if any of these occur:

  • medical services are offered to the public without a CQC scope assessment;
  • diagnostics or procedures are added without identifying the regulated activity and provider;
  • no written contract identifies clinical control for outsourced services;
  • clinicians work without verified professional registration, indemnity, or scope of practice;
  • coaches or executives override clinical return-to-play decisions;
  • records are incomplete, inaccessible, or mixed into performance systems without controls;
  • incidents are discussed informally but not logged, investigated, or closed;
  • academy or child-player care lacks safeguarding and consent controls;
  • premises or emergency equipment checks lapse.

docs.title: Implementation roadmap
docs.text:
1. Create a complete inventory of all medical services, locations, providers, and user groups.
  2. Run the registration decision model for each service.
  3. Put immediate controls around any public, diagnostic, prescribing, remote-triage, injection, IV, or clinic-like activity.
  4. Approve a medical service statement of purpose.
  5. Establish the medical governance committee and risk register.
  6. Build the evidence pack and dashboard.
  7. Review contractor contracts and registration/exemption evidence.
  8. Audit records, consent, concussion, equipment, and clinician credentials.
  9. Report quarterly to the board and annually refresh the CQC scope analysis.

Use Assurance Pack Templates for the practical document set. If implementing these routines in software, use Platform Product Blueprint to translate the operating model into workflows, evidence stores, dashboards, and export packs.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:

  • Medicines, Prescribing, and Anti-Doping gives the controls for the medicines risk line in the dashboard.
  • Registration Decision Model keeps new services from drifting across the registration boundary unnoticed.
  • Platform Product Blueprint maps the operating model to potential software modules and external tool categories.

docs.title: Why this is high risk
docs.text:
Medicines sit at the sharpest interface between CQC governance, professional duties, player welfare, and anti-doping. A club medical department may hold emergency medicines, prescribe or administer pain relief, use injections, manage asthma medication, advise on supplements, handle concussion medication restrictions, and support Therapeutic Use Exemption (TUE) applications through UKAD's medicine and TUE guidance.

The governance aim is simple: no medicine should be prescribed, supplied, stored, administered, reported, or disclosed unless the club can explain the clinical indication, professional authority, anti-doping status, consent, record, and follow-up.

docs.title: CQC medicines optimisation standard
docs.text:
CQC's medicines optimisation quality statement expects medicines and treatments to be safe and to meet people's needs, capacities, and preferences. The statement covers involvement in medicines decisions, best practice, lawful prescribing/supply/administration, accurate medicines information during transfers of care, and controlled-drugs oversight.

For a Rugby League club this means:

  • medicines are clinically indicated, not used to serve selection or convenience;
  • players understand risks, benefits, alternatives, anti-doping status, and follow-up;
  • prescribing is within professional scope and based on adequate assessment;
  • regular and acute medicines are recorded in the clinical record;
  • medicines information follows the player across club, hospital, GP, specialist, loan, and national-team pathways where lawful and necessary;
  • emergency medicines are checked, in date, stored appropriately, and reviewed after use;
  • controlled drugs or medicines liable to abuse/misuse have additional safeguards.

docs.title: RFL anti-doping controls
docs.text:
The RFL Medical Standards 2026 require medical staff to be aware of anti-doping rules and the WADA Prohibited List. They make checking medication and supplements mandatory. They direct use of Global DRO through UKAD's Search Check Apply process for medicines and highlight that supplements cannot be checked the same way because they are not licensed medicines.

Professional players are treated as being in the UKAD National TUE Pool under the RFL standards, so TUE timing matters. If a medication contains a prohibited substance and there is clinical need, a TUE may be needed before use except in emergencies or defined retroactive scenarios. The RFL's anti-doping page is the sport-specific reference point.

docs.title: 1. Medicines formulary and scope
docs.text:
Maintain a club medicines formulary:

  • emergency medicines required by RFL mandatory equipment/drugs box;
  • medicines carried by the club doctor;
  • medicines supplied or administered by physiotherapists or other clinicians;
  • medicines held for travel;
  • oxygen and medical gases;
  • topical products, wound-care products, local anaesthetic, analgesia, asthma medication, glucose products, and cardiac/emergency medicines;
  • controlled drugs or high-risk medicines, if any.

Each medicine should state:

  • indication;
  • legal classification;
  • who may prescribe, supply, administer, or support self-administration;
  • storage condition;
  • expiry check frequency;
  • documentation requirement;
  • anti-doping check requirement;
  • TUE implication;
  • emergency escalation route.

docs.title: 2. Prescribing and administration controls
docs.text:
Minimum controls:

  • prescribe only after adequate assessment and access to relevant medical history where needed, consistent with GMC prescribing guidance;
  • check allergy status, current medication, contraindications, and interaction risk;
  • check Global DRO before prescribing or advising use where anti-doping applies;
  • check whether the player needs a TUE using UKAD guidance or the TUE Wizard;
  • document dose, route, batch where relevant, indication, consent, advice, and follow-up;
  • inform the player's GP or regular clinician where necessary and with appropriate consent;
  • review medicines after emergency use, adverse effects, or transfer of care;
  • report adverse drug reactions and device incidents through appropriate clinical governance and MHRA Yellow Card routes where relevant.

docs.title: 3. Anti-doping decision flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  A["Medication or treatment proposed"] --> B["Is it clinically necessary?"]
  B -- "No" --> C["Do not use; consider non-drug alternatives"]
  B -- "Yes" --> D["Search medication on Global DRO using correct country and formulation"]
  D --> E{"Status"}
  E -- "Permitted" --> F["Document search reference, consent, prescription/admin record"]
  E -- "Conditional" --> G["Check limits, route, dose, timing, in/out of competition, washout"]
  E -- "Prohibited" --> H["Use UKAD TUE Wizard / seek UKAD or governing body advice"]
  G --> I{"Within conditions?"}
  I -- "Yes" --> F
  I -- "No" --> H
  H --> J{"Emergency?"}
  J -- "Yes" --> K["Treat first; compile medical file for retroactive TUE if needed"]
  J -- "No" --> L["Apply for TUE before use; consider permitted alternatives"]

docs.title: IV products and injections
docs.text:
This is a red-flag area for both CQC and anti-doping:

  • CQC can treat IV administration of prescription-only or wellness products as registrable treatment where it is delivered by or under a listed healthcare professional and claimed to alter physiological state.
  • RFL standards state that intravenous infusions are prohibited at all times except in specified legitimate medical situations such as emergency intervention, blood replacement, surgical procedures, or administration where other routes are unavailable, and not for exercise-induced dehydration.
  • Injections may be permitted as a method if the substance is not prohibited and volume limits are met, but the clinical governance and anti-doping checks still apply.

Club control:

  • no IV wellness, recovery, hydration, or performance service without board approval, CQC scope review, UKAD review, and medical governance sign-off;
  • every injection must have indication, consent, anti-doping check, route, dose, volume, batch where relevant, clinician, and follow-up recorded;
  • any glucocorticoid, beta-2 agonist, opioid analgesic, stimulant, hormone, or controlled drug should trigger enhanced review.

docs.title: Supplements
docs.text:
UKAD warns in its supplement risk guidance that no supplement can be guaranteed free of prohibited substances. A club should:

  • require documented need assessment before supplement use;
  • prefer food-first nutrition and clinician/dietitian review;
  • use batch-tested products where supplements are used;
  • record batch number, supplier, date, player, rationale, and risk discussion;
  • make clear that strict liability remains with the athlete;
  • avoid coaches, sponsors, or commercial partners introducing supplements outside the medical/nutrition governance route.

docs.title: Concussion-specific medication control
docs.text:
The RFL standards require doctors to consider whether pharmacological agents or medicines may mask or modify concussion symptoms when determining symptom-free status. Governance controls:

  • record all medication taken after head injury;
  • advise against medicines that mask deterioration unless clinically justified;
  • include medication review before GRTP progression;
  • include medication status on return-to-play sign-off;
  • document player advice about driving, alcohol, sedating medication, and escalation.

docs.title: Board assurance questions
docs.text:
• Is there a named medicines lead?
  • Is the mandatory drugs box complete, checked, in date, and reconciled?
  • Are controlled drugs present? If yes, is the Home Office and governance position clear?
  • Are all prescribing clinicians working within scope and indemnity?
  • Are Global DRO checks and TUE decisions recorded?
  • Are emergency medicines reviewed after use?
  • Are adverse incidents and near misses reviewed?
  • Are players given enough information to make informed choices?
  • Are supplement risks governed through medical/nutrition, not performance pressure?

docs.text:

  • RFL Medical Standards Map identifies the RFL controls that feed into medicines assurance.
  • Assurance Pack Templates includes a medicines and anti-doping log.
