docs.title: Minimum Compliance Pack Research
docs.text:
This page records the research basis for a minimum compliance pack. It is not yet a final implementation pack and should not be treated as a product specification.

The research question is: what evidence would a professional Rugby League club reasonably need to hold to show that its medical service is governed, whether or not CQC registration is required?

Use this page with Assurance Pack Templates, Registration Decision Model, RFL Clause Evidence Map, and CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements.

The scenario validation work is recorded separately in Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix to keep this page readable.

docs.title: Research boundary
docs.text:
The pack should support three situations:

  1. the club concludes that the service is outside CQC registration or excluded because it is sports-ground/event treatment for participants or attendees;
  2. the club uses an external provider and needs evidence that the provider, rather than the club, carries on any regulated activity;
  3. the club identifies possible registration risk and needs a controlled evidence pack before taking legal/CQC advice.

The pack should not assume that every club must register with CQC. It should instead preserve the facts, evidence, and reasoning used to reach the conclusion.

docs.title: Source anchors
docs.text: Source Research implication CQC scope of registration The pack must answer activity, provider/control, exception, location, and registration route questions. CQC treatment of disease, disorder or injury Sports-ground/event treatment exclusions are central, but wider clinics, diagnostics, prescribing, IV services, public access, or non-sport services can change the analysis. CQC evidence categories Evidence should be organised by people's experience, staff/leaders, partners, observation, processes, and outcomes. RFL Medical Standards 2026 RFL medical requirements create the sport-specific evidence layer for staff, EAP, equipment, records, anti-doping, concussion, cardiac screening, and policies. CQC Regulation 17: Good governance Even where registration is not required, governance evidence should show risk management, audit, records, and action closure.

docs.title: Candidate pack inventory
docs.text: Pack component Research purpose Current evidence hypothesis Validation still needed CQC scope decision record Preserve why the club thinks registration is or is not required. Should capture service model, user group, activities, clinicians, exclusions, provider/control, location, reviewer, date, and triggers for review. Test with regulatory counsel and at least one real Championship club service model. Medical service description Define what the club medical service is and is not. Should resemble a statement-of-purpose discipline even where CQC registration is not required. Confirm board and Head of Medical language that is usable without implying registered-provider status. RFL standards evidence dashboard Show whether sport-specific obligations are evidenced. Should map to A1-F11 in RFL Clause Evidence Map. Confirm Championship-specific requirements and distinguish mandatory, best practice, and information-only clauses. Clinical governance meeting record Show medical oversight, decisions, actions, and escalation. Should record incidents, audit results, records, confidentiality, equipment, EAP, concussion, medicines, contractors, and speaking-up issues. Validate cadence and attendance with club medical leads. Contractor due diligence file Show who carries on any healthcare activity and what assurance the club receives. Should include registration/exemption analysis, contracts, credentials, incident reporting, audit rights, records, medicines, and quality reports. Test against cardiac screening, ambulance/event cover, diagnostics, and external doctor models. Incident and serious injury record Preserve response, notifications, learning, and governance closure. Should include RFL reporting, CQC notification/duty-of-candour review where relevant, safeguarding, anti-doping/TUE flags, and action closure. Align terminology with RFL serious injury/death reporting and club safeguarding process. Concussion assurance record Evidence baseline, recognise-and-remove, HIA, GRTP, reporting, and RTP controls. Should use RFL Section E as the source control set. Confirm competition-specific timing and submission evidence for Championship context. Medicines and anti-doping log Connect clinical medicines decisions to UKAD/TUE controls. Should record prescriber, medicine, indication, route, dose, consent, allergy, Global DRO/UKAD check, TUE status, batch/expiry where relevant, and follow-up. Review with prescribing clinician and anti-doping lead. Board medical assurance report Give directors assurance without exposing confidential clinical detail. Should summarise scope status, RFL compliance, incidents, audit, overdue risks, contractor assurance, and decisions needed. Define what board can see, what Head of Medical retains, and what needs anonymisation. Annual medical governance review Close the loop across the season. Should review CQC scope, RFL compliance, incidents, records, medicines, concussion, cardiac screening, contractors, facilities, player experience, and action plans. Test against a club board calendar and season structure.

docs.title: Evidence categories to preserve
docs.text:
The pack should deliberately avoid relying only on policies. For each component, research should identify evidence across CQC's categories:

Evidence category Sports medical examples People's experience player feedback, consent concerns, confidentiality concerns, complaint themes, post-concussion welfare experience. Staff and leaders clinician feedback, Head of Medical assurance, board minutes, speaking-up issues, locum debriefs. Partners RFL, UKAD, ambulance provider, hospital, external screening provider, academy/schools, parent club for loan players. Observation treatment-room audit, equipment checks, match-day briefing observation, EAP rehearsal. Processes policies, SOPs, scope decision records, incident workflows, medicine checks, GRTP pathway. Outcomes concussion compliance, equipment exceptions, record completeness, incident closure, screening completion, overdue actions.

docs.title: Scenario validation status
docs.text:
The candidate pack has now been tested against six practical service patterns in Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix:

Scenario Research finding Player-only match/training medical service Supports a core club medical governance pack, provided the participant/event boundary is evidenced. External cardiac screening provider Requires contractor and diagnostics appendix because RFL compliance does not settle the CQC provider/control position. Public/private clinic or paid treatment risk Sits outside the routine minimum pack and requires specialist CQC/legal assessment before launch. Remote triage/advice Requires separate analysis because ordinary follow-up and formal remote triage are materially different. Concussion and return-to-play pathway Supports a dedicated concussion appendix with strict clinical confidentiality boundaries. Contractor-led ambulance/event medical cover Requires contractor/event-medical appendix and explicit command-and-control evidence.

The main research conclusion is that the minimum pack should be split into a core governance layer plus scenario appendices, rather than one oversized universal document.

docs.title: Research-complete definition
docs.text:
Item 2 should only be marked research-complete when:

  • every candidate pack component has a source basis;
  • each component has a clear evidence owner and review cadence;
  • confidential clinical data and board-visible assurance have been separated;
  • the pack has been tested against at least six scenarios: player-only medical service, external cardiac screening, public/private clinic risk, remote triage/advice, concussion and return-to-play, and contractor-led ambulance/event medical cover;
  • regulatory/legal review points are marked clearly rather than implied.

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Which pack components are genuinely minimum for a Championship club, and which are best-practice extras?
  • How much evidence does the board need to hold versus receive as anonymised assurance?
  • Should academy/scholarship arrangements use the same pack or a child-specific variant?
  • How should the pack deal with clubs that already use sports EMR, safeguarding, HR, or incident systems?
  • What evidence would a CQC assessor, RFL reviewer, insurer, or claimant's lawyer actually ask for first?

docs.title: Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix
docs.text:
This page tests the Minimum Compliance Pack Research against six practical club scenarios. It is still research, not a final product design.

The purpose is to identify:

  • what evidence appears necessary;
  • which CQC/RFL source creates or supports the need;
  • who would usually own the evidence;
  • what the board can see without accessing confidential clinical detail;
  • what remains uncertain and needs legal, clinical, RFL, or operational validation.

docs.title: Summary matrix
docs.text: Scenario Core pack components Specialist appendices Research status Player-only match/training medical service Scope decision record; medical service description; RFL dashboard; governance meeting record; board assurance report; annual review. Clinical records audit; equipment/EAP checks; medicines; concussion; safeguarding. Baseline evidence need is clear; competition-specific RFL detail still needs validation. External cardiac screening provider Scope decision record; contractor due diligence file; RFL dashboard; board assurance report. Diagnostic/screening provider file; abnormal-result pathway; player communication record. Strong need for provider/control evidence; CQC registration or exemption position must be checked for the provider model. Public/private clinic or paid treatment risk Full CQC scope decision record; service description; board decision paper; legal/CQC advice file. Registration application pack if required; complaints; duty of candour; staffing; premises; clinical governance. High-risk scenario; do not treat as minimum routine pack without specialist advice. Remote triage/advice Scope decision record; service description; clinical safety-netting process; records/data protection file. Remote advice/triage assessment; response-time audit; safeguarding/parental consent where academy players are included. Needs careful factual distinction between ordinary follow-up and a formal remote advice/triage service. Concussion and return-to-play pathway RFL dashboard; incident record; concussion assurance record; clinical governance meeting record; board assurance report. HIA reports; GRTP; RTP form; Cognigram/SCAT6; specialist reports. Strong RFL source basis; board-visible evidence must avoid unnecessary identifiable clinical detail. Contractor-led ambulance/event medical cover Scope decision record; contractor due diligence file; EAP; pre-match briefing; incident route; board assurance report. CQC transport/ambulance analysis; command-and-control record; post-event quality report. Provider/control and transport-to-hospital boundary require validation before relying on contractor status.

docs.title: Scenario 1: Player-only match/training medical service
docs.text:
This is the baseline professional club service: match-day and training-ground care for contracted players and other eligible participants, not a public clinic.

Evidence requirement Source or rationale Usual owner Visibility Research uncertainty CQC scope decision record showing participant-only service, sports premises/event context, and no public/private treatment. CQC treatment guidance includes sports-ground/gym and temporary sporting-event exclusions. Head of Medical with accountable executive. Board-visible summary; full rationale retained by governance lead. Need to validate how associated premises and academy/trialist groups are described for each club. Medical service description defining who is served and what is not provided. CQC scope depends on facts; statement-of-purpose discipline helps prevent service creep. Head of Medical. Board-visible. Language must avoid implying registration if the club is not registered. RFL standards evidence dashboard for staff, EAP, equipment, records, concussion, medicines, anti-doping, cardiac where applicable. RFL Medical Standards 2026. Head of Medical. Board-visible as compliance status and exceptions. Exact mandatory/best-practice status by competition still needs checking. Clinical records audit and confidentiality control. CQC evidence categories and good governance logic; RFL note-keeping requirements. Head of Medical and data protection lead. Board-visible as anonymised completeness/exception data; individual records restricted. Need to confirm current record systems used by clubs. Annual medical governance review. Regulation 17-style governance discipline and RFL assurance. Board medical lead or accountable executive. Board-visible. Frequency and committee route need validation with club governance calendars.

Minimum pack placement: core pack.

docs.title: Scenario 2: External cardiac screening provider
docs.text:
The club commissions an external screening provider to screen players, often because the RFL Medical Standards make cardiac screening mandatory for specified competitions including Championship.

Evidence requirement Source or rationale Usual owner Visibility Research uncertainty Provider due diligence file, including legal entity, CQC registration or exemption rationale, clinician credentials, indemnity, records, results, and escalation. CQC diagnostic and screening procedures may apply depending on who performs, analyses, reports, and controls the pathway. Head of Medical with operations/procurement. Board-visible as provider status and assurance; contract details may be restricted. Need provider-specific registration/exemption review. Contract defining clinical control, reporting, abnormal-result pathway, record ownership, confidentiality, incident reporting, and audit rights. CQC provider/control analysis and contractor governance. Club operations/procurement with Head of Medical. Board-visible summary; full contract restricted. Shared-control models need legal review. Attendance/completion dashboard. RFL cardiac screening compliance and board assurance. Head of Medical. Board-visible as completion percentage and overdue exceptions. Need to define participation gate if screening incomplete. Abnormal-result pathway and follow-up evidence. Safe/effective care and diagnostic governance. Club doctor or screening provider, depending on model. Board-visible only as anonymised risk/action status; identifiable clinical results restricted. Need to confirm who communicates results and who owns onward referral. Scope decision record distinguishing club commissioning from club carrying on screening. CQC scope and provider/control logic. Accountable executive with Head of Medical. Board-visible conclusion; detailed legal rationale restricted. This is the main uncertainty for each provider model.

Minimum pack placement: core pack plus contractor/diagnostics appendix.

docs.title: Scenario 3: Public/private clinic or paid treatment risk
docs.text:
This covers a club offering paid physiotherapy, sports medicine, diagnostics, injections, or treatment to the public, sponsors, staff families, schools, or community users outside the participant/event context.

Evidence requirement Source or rationale Usual owner Visibility Research uncertainty Full CQC scope decision record before launch. CQC scope guidance and treatment guidance; sports exclusion may not apply to wider public/private services. Chief executive/accountable executive with Head of Medical. Board-visible. High likelihood of needing legal/CQC advice. Service description, user group, payment model, activities, premises, clinicians, records, complaints, incidents, medicines, and provider/control model. Registration depends on the facts, not the club label. Project sponsor and Head of Medical. Board-visible in summary; clinical detail restricted. The model may shift into independent healthcare provider territory. Board decision record showing approval, risk, advice, and launch conditions. Good governance and provider accountability. Board/company secretary. Board-visible. Board may need to pause launch until CQC position is settled. Registration readiness pack if required. CQC registration requirements may include regulated activities, locations, nominated individual, registered manager, policies, and evidence. Accountable executive. Board-visible status; application documents controlled. Specialist regulatory advice needed. Complaints, duty-of-candour, safeguarding, staffing, premises, medicines, and records framework. Independent healthcare governance pattern. Registered/provider governance lead if applicable. Board-visible as assurance; individual cases restricted. Depends on whether service is launched and under what legal entity.

Minimum pack placement: specialist high-risk pack, not routine minimum pack.

docs.title: Scenario 4: Remote triage/advice
docs.text:
This covers a formal remote app, hotline, or advice pathway, especially if it prioritises urgent needs, gives immediate advice, or extends beyond contracted players.

Evidence requirement Source or rationale Usual owner Visibility Research uncertainty Scope decision record distinguishing ordinary follow-up from a formal remote advice/triage service. CQC transport, triage, and medical advice provided remotely guidance. Head of Medical with accountable executive. Board-visible conclusion. The boundary is fact-sensitive and needs careful review. Eligibility rules and service description. User group and activity determine registration risk. Head of Medical and service owner. Board-visible. Academy, parent, community, or public access changes the risk. Clinical safety-netting and escalation process. Safe care and treatment; remote advice risk management. Clinical governance lead. Board-visible as protocol status; individual advice records restricted. Need clinician validation and possible clinical safety review. Record, response-time, and audit process. Evidence of safe, consistent remote advice and outcomes. Head of Medical. Board-visible as aggregate audit; individual records restricted. Current systems may not support structured remote advice records. Data protection, consent, safeguarding, and parental consent model where minors are included. Health data and safeguarding duties. Data protection lead and safeguarding lead. Board-visible as assurance; case details restricted. Child/academy use may require separate pathway.

Minimum pack placement: core pack only if limited follow-up; specialist remote-advice appendix if formal service.

docs.title: Scenario 5: Concussion and return-to-play pathway
docs.text:
This covers suspected concussion, HIA, post-match care, GRTP, reporting, return-to-play, loan-player handover, and specialist referral.

Evidence requirement Source or rationale Usual owner Visibility Research uncertainty Baseline completion dashboard. RFL concussion requirements for baseline assessments. Head of Medical. Board-visible as completion and exceptions. Need competition-specific baseline requirements confirmed. HIA/concussion report, removal record, and post-injury advice. RFL Section E and safe care evidence. Match-day doctor/club doctor. Individual report restricted; board sees aggregate compliance and exceptions. Need exact submission evidence and timing by competition. GRTP log and doctor clearance evidence. RFL GRTP requirements and player safety. Club doctor. Restricted clinical record; board-visible as compliant/not compliant and overdue risks. Need clear rule on board visibility when selection pressure exists. Return-to-play form, Cognigram/SCAT6, specialist reports where required. RFL reporting and return-to-play controls. Club doctor. Restricted; anonymised board exception reporting. Specialist referral thresholds need validation. Clinical governance review of misses, late reports, symptoms masked by medication, and loan-player notification issues. CQC learning culture and RFL reporting assurance. Head of Medical. Board-visible as themes/actions. Need alignment with league reporting and parent-club protocols.

Minimum pack placement: core pack plus concussion appendix.

docs.title: Scenario 6: Contractor-led ambulance/event medical cover
docs.text:
This covers a club contracting external event medical cover, paramedics, ambulance capability, first aiders, or pitch-side support for home fixtures.

Evidence requirement Source or rationale Usual owner Visibility Research uncertainty Contractor due diligence file including legal entity, CQC registration or exemption analysis, clinical lead, indemnity, staff credentials, incident route, and quality reports. CQC provider/control and transport activity guidance. Operations/procurement with Head of Medical. Board-visible status; detailed credentials and contract restricted as needed. Contractor registration may not cover every activity or location. Command-and-control record between club clinicians and contractor. Provider/control clarity and emergency planning. Head of Medical and match operations. Board-visible as approved model. Shared control is the main risk. Emergency action plan and pre-match briefing record. RFL EAP and pre-match briefing requirements. Home club lead clinician and operations. Board-visible completion status; operational details restricted where needed. Venue changes and temporary arrangements need review. Equipment, medicines, ambulance, communication, and access checks. RFL equipment and emergency standards; safe care. Contractor clinical lead and club match operations. Board-visible as pass/fail exceptions. Need clarity on who owns each check. Incident, serious injury, complaint, safeguarding, CQC notification, and post-event report route. Good governance, CQC/RFL reporting, and contractor assurance. Head of Medical and contractor lead. Board-visible themes/actions; identifiable clinical detail restricted. Need to validate duty-of-candour/CQC route where contractor is registered provider.

Minimum pack placement: core pack plus contractor/event-medical appendix.

docs.title: Cross-scenario evidence boundaries
docs.text: Evidence type Board-visible Restricted clinical/governance record Scope decision Conclusion, date, reviewer, key conditions, triggers for review. Detailed legal advice, disputed facts, provider-control analysis where sensitive. RFL compliance Completion status, overdue actions, exceptions, risk rating. Individual player records, clinical assessments, identifiable health information. Concussion Compliance rate, overdue GRTP/RTP exceptions, learning actions. HIA forms, Cognigram/SCAT6 results, clinical notes, specialist reports. Cardiac screening Provider status, completion rate, abnormal-result pathway assurance. Individual results, detailed reports, referral records. Medicines/anti-doping Audit status, incident themes, TUE process assurance. Player medication records, indications, TUE medical evidence. Contractors Registration/exemption status, contract assurance, quality report themes. Full clinical records and provider confidential information unless needed for assurance.

docs.title: Research conclusion for item 2
docs.text:
The minimum pack appears to need two layers:

  1. Core club medical governance pack: scope decision, service description, RFL dashboard, clinical governance record, incident/complaint route, contractor register, board assurance report, annual review.
  2. Scenario appendices: diagnostics/cardiac screening, public/private clinic, remote triage/advice, concussion, medicines/anti-doping, contractor/event medical cover, academy/children.

This supports a research conclusion, not a final specification: the pack can be defined only after validating which scenarios are active for the club and which are merely future-risk triggers.

docs.title: Platform MVP and Guardrails
docs.text:
This page sets out implementation choices and risk controls for a sports medical governance and CQC-readiness platform.

Use it with Product Research Roadmap, Platform Opportunity, Platform Tool Landscape, and Platform Product Blueprint.

docs.title: Build, buy, or integrate
docs.text:
Strategy When it fits Risks Build a specialist sports CQC platform Best if the product aims to own the CQC/RFL governance layer and serve clubs across sports. Must manage clinical data, information governance, clinical safety, and possible medical-device boundary. Integrate with athlete EMR systems Best where clubs already use Kitman Labs, Smartabase/Teamworks, AthleteMonitoring, SportsWare, or Orreco for medical records. Need reliable APIs, permissions, and evidence mapping without duplicating records. Integrate with CQC compliance tools Best where a provider already uses Radar Healthcare, Vantage, QCS, or similar for wider healthcare compliance. These tools may not understand sports workflows, RFL standards, or participant-only exclusions. Start as documentation/evidence layer Best for MVP: decision model, document pack, audits, evidence vault, board reporting. Less useful if clubs need daily clinical record capture from day one. Start as digital recording tool Best if the immediate pain is match-day/training-ground records, incident logs, and medicines. Without the CQC evidence layer it becomes another sports medical record product.

The most pragmatic MVP is a governance and evidence layer with lightweight record capture, designed to integrate with specialist athlete EMR systems later.

docs.title: Proposed MVP
docs.text:
1. CQC scope decision workflow using the Registration Decision Model.
  2. Stock documentation pack based on Assurance Pack Templates.
  3. Evidence vault mapped to CQC key questions, evidence categories, and RFL medical standards.
  4. Incident and complaint workflow with CQC, safeguarding, and RFL flags.
  5. Medicines and anti-doping log with UKAD/Global DRO reference fields.
  6. Workforce credentials register for medical staff, contractors, and role privileges.
  7. Board dashboard and export pack for the Head of Medical and board of directors.

docs.title: CQC framework versioning
docs.text:
CQC assessment terminology and scoring are changing. The platform should version its CQC mappings by date and source, and preserve the evidence basis for each mapping. Do not hard-code one static set of quality statements as if it will remain unchanged.

Relevant sources include CQC evidence categories, CQC assessment, and CQC's consultation on improving how it assesses and rates providers.

docs.title: Health data and confidentiality
docs.text:
Player medical data is special category health data. The ICO guidance on special category data explicitly includes information about injury, diagnosis, treatment, test results, medical opinions, and future health status.

The product should support:

  • role-based access;
  • audit logs;
  • coach-facing summaries separated from clinical records;
  • consent records;
  • retention rules;
  • subject access and export processes;
  • data-controller and processor records for club, contractor, league, and platform roles.

docs.title: Clinical safety and medical-device boundary
docs.text:
If the platform only records, organises, and reports information, it may remain a governance/records system. If it starts recommending diagnosis, treatment, return-to-play decisions, risk stratification, or clinical action, the product may need clinical safety and medical-device analysis.

Relevant guardrails include NHS DCB0129 clinical risk management, NHS clinical safety developer guidance, and MHRA software and AI as a medical device guidance.

docs.title: Registration boundary is a workflow, not a badge
docs.text:
The platform should not label a service "CQC compliant" as a static status. It should maintain a living evidence trail:

  • what service was assessed;
  • which source guidance was used;
  • what facts were relied on;
  • who approved the conclusion;
  • when it must be reviewed;
  • what service changes invalidate the conclusion.

docs.title: Research still needed
docs.text:
• Interview Heads of Medical at Championship, Super League, women's, academy, and community clubs.
  • Compare current use of athlete EMR systems in Rugby League and football clubs.
  • Test whether clubs want an independent platform or an evidence layer that integrates with existing systems.
  • Validate minimum viable document pack with sports regulatory lawyers and CQC specialists.
  • Define data-controller and processor models for club, contractor, league, and platform.
  • Assess whether any proposed decision-support features would trigger MHRA software-as-medical-device obligations.
  • Refine the first-pass RFL Clause Evidence Map into product controls and evidence fields.
  • Build a sample board dashboard using the Board Medical Assurance Report.

docs.title: Platform Opportunity
docs.text:
This page is the hub for product research on an online platform that helps professional sports teams understand CQC exposure, maintain CQC-style governance evidence, and run the day-to-day records, audits, and assurance workflows that support safe medical services.

It is intentionally short. The detailed tool landscape, product blueprint, MVP, and digital-health guardrails have been split into linked pages so board, clinical, product, and engineering readers can move directly to the material they need.

docs.title: Why split the platform research?
docs.text:
The content design guidance used for this wiki does not set a universal maximum page length. GOV.UK says page length depends on the content and user need, but warns against broad "everything you need to know" pages because they are hard to navigate. Nielsen Norman Group research, summarised by Newcastle University's digital design guidance, also reinforces that people usually scan web pages rather than reading every word.

For this wiki, the practical rule is:

  • keep hub pages short;
  • split long research into task-based pages;
  • keep each page focused on one reader need;
  • cross-link pages so the LLM output and human navigation both preserve context.

docs.title: Product hypothesis
docs.text:
There is a gap between three existing software markets:

  1. CQC compliance platforms are strong at policies, audits, evidence stores, and inspection readiness, but are usually designed for care homes, clinics, hospitals, primary care, or social care rather than professional sport.
  2. Sports medicine and athlete management systems are strong at injury, illness, rehabilitation, availability, and performance medicine records, but usually do not map records to CQC regulated activities, evidence categories, or CQC assessment response packs.
  3. Sports governance and safeguarding tools are strong at vetting, safeguarding, incident reporting, concussion workflows, and safe sport governance, but usually do not provide private healthcare registration logic or CQC evidence mapping.

The product opportunity is a sports medical governance and CQC-readiness platform: a system that keeps club medical operations sport-specific while making CQC evidence, records, and decision-making explicit where the club approaches regulated healthcare.

docs.title: Platform Product Blueprint
docs.text:
This page maps CQC evidence and Rugby League medical governance into possible platform modules.

Use it with Product Research Roadmap, Platform Tool Landscape, Assurance Pack Templates, and Governance Operating Model.

docs.title: CQC evidence model the platform should support
docs.text:
CQC's current provider assessment approach uses five key questions and evidence categories. CQC describes six evidence categories: people's experience, feedback from staff and leaders, feedback from partners, observation, processes, and outcomes.

The product should not simply store files. It should map operational evidence to the relevant evidence category, CQC domain, service model, location, team category, and club risk.

CQC evidence category Sports medical evidence examples Platform feature People's experience Player feedback, academy parent feedback, complaint themes, consent and confidentiality feedback. Player-safe feedback forms, confidential surveys, complaint log, trend dashboard. Feedback from staff and leaders Head of Medical reports, clinician speaking-up records, staff debriefs, board medical assurance minutes. Structured debriefs, board report builder, staff survey, speaking-up workflow. Feedback from partners Contractor quality reports, ambulance/venue feedback, NHS/private clinic referral feedback, league/club welfare feedback. Contractor portal, partner evidence upload, referral outcome tracker. Observation Match-day medical room checks, training-ground audits, emergency action plan drills, equipment observations. Mobile audit checklist, photo evidence, drill records, corrective actions. Processes Policies, SOPs, service scope decisions, clinical records, medicines logs, CQC registration analyses. Policy library, version control, decision model, clinical forms, medicines/TUE workflow. Outcomes Injury trends, return-to-play delays, concussion compliance, incident recurrence, audit closure, complaint response times. Dashboards, outcome metrics, exception reporting, board assurance packs.

This links directly to CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements and the Board Medical Assurance Report.

docs.title: 1. Registration and scope decision engine
docs.text:
The platform should guide the club through the Registration Decision Model, not replace expert judgement. It should record:

  • user group: players, academy, staff, officials, public, schools, sponsors;
  • location: stadium, training ground, away fixture, mobile unit, third-party premises, remote service;
  • activity: treatment, diagnostics, screening, prescribing, injections, remote triage, transport, rehabilitation, mental health;
  • sports-ground or sporting-event exclusion analysis;
  • provider/control analysis: club, contractor, joint service, registered provider;
  • decision outcome: outside scope, sports exclusion, contractor registered, contractor exempt, likely club registration, legal/CQC advice needed.

The decision output should populate the CQC scope assessment template, produce a board-readable summary, and trigger periodic reassessment when the service changes.

docs.title: 2. Adaptable documentation library
docs.text:
The platform should include stock documents that are adaptable by sport, league, team type, and service model:

  • statement of purpose;
  • medical governance policy;
  • clinical records policy;
  • incident and serious event policy;
  • complaints policy;
  • safeguarding clinical interface policy;
  • medicines, prescribing, controlled drugs, and anti-doping policy;
  • emergency action plan template;
  • contractor due diligence checklist;
  • data protection and confidentiality policy;
  • remote advice and triage SOP;
  • diagnostics and screening governance SOP.

These should be version-controlled and linked to evidence categories, regulations, RFL obligations, and board approval dates. The templates in Assurance Pack Templates are the first minimum content set.

docs.title: 3. Digital clinical and sports medical records
docs.text:
A sports team needs structured clinical records without turning coach-facing performance notes into unrestricted health records. Useful modules include:

  • injury and illness record;
  • pitchside assessment and treatment note;
  • training-ground treatment note;
  • SOAP note;
  • concussion/HIA/return-to-play record;
  • rehabilitation and restriction plan;
  • referral and diagnostics tracker;
  • medication and anti-doping/TUE log;
  • consent and information-sharing preferences;
  • loan-player and transfer handover record;
  • academy parental communication log.

The product should respect the confidentiality risks described in Medicines, Prescribing, and Anti-Doping and should separate clinical notes from coach/performance availability summaries.

docs.title: 4. Evidence vault and CQC pack builder
docs.text:
The platform should let a club assemble evidence by CQC domain, evidence category, regulated activity candidate, location, team category, season, board reporting period, and incident/audit action.

Outputs should include:

  • CQC registration readiness pack;
  • CQC assessment evidence pack;
  • CQC factual accuracy response bundle;
  • board medical assurance report;
  • contractor assurance pack;
  • annual governance review pack.

This is the productised version of Governance Operating Model and Assurance Pack Templates.

docs.title: 5. Incident, complaint, safeguarding, and duty-of-candour workflow
docs.text:
The platform should combine sport incident reporting with healthcare-style governance:

  • injury, medicines, equipment, safeguarding, data, complaint, contractor, and emergency-transfer categories;
  • immediate action and escalation;
  • root cause analysis;
  • action plan and closure evidence;
  • RFL reporting flag;
  • CQC notification/duty-of-candour review flag;
  • safeguarding lead notification;
  • board-level trend reporting.

This is where sports governance tools show strong patterns, but the CQC layer would add healthcare governance and provider evidence mapping.

docs.title: 6. Workforce, credentials, training, and privileges
docs.text:
The platform should maintain professional registration, role and scope, indemnity, DBS/safeguarding checks where relevant, RFL medical role requirements, IMMOFP/iIMMOFP evidence, concussion training, emergency care qualifications, anti-doping education, and medicines permissions.

This connects RFL Medical Standards Map with CQC staffing, fit-person, and well-led assurance.

docs.title: 7. Medicines, anti-doping, and therapeutic use exemptions
docs.text:
This module should include medicine administration and prescribing logs, allergy checks, Global DRO/UKAD check references, TUE flags, controlled-drug controls, batch/expiry records, medicines incident reporting, audit, stock checks, player advice, and follow-up records.

The Medicines and anti-doping log is the minimum viable record model.

docs.title: Platform Tool Landscape
docs.text:
This page compares the software markets that overlap with a possible sports medical governance and CQC-readiness platform.

Use it with Product Research Roadmap, Platform Opportunity, Platform Product Blueprint, and Platform MVP and Guardrails.

This page records roadmap item 6: deepen market and tool research. It should be read as market learning, not a build decision. The more detailed capability comparison is in Tool Capability Comparison.

docs.title: Market map
docs.text: Segment Examples Strong capabilities Limits for Rugby League CQC use CQC compliance and quality platforms Agilio TeamNet for independent healthcare, Radar Healthcare CQC Compliance Software, Vantage Compliance and Quality Management, QCS CQC-aligned evidence, policies, audits, document control, action plans, incident/risk modules, dashboards, inspection readiness. Not built around sports-ground exclusions, participant-only care, RFL medical standards, anti-doping, athlete confidentiality, loan players, match-day/training-ground records, or dual clinical/performance reporting. Sports medicine EMR and athlete management Kitman Labs Performance Medicine, Teamworks AMS / Smartabase, AthleteMonitoring EMR, SportsWareOnLine, Orreco Medical Notes Athlete health records, injury and illness logs, SOAP notes, return-to-play, concussion, availability, diagnostics, medication/TUE tracking, performance data integration. Usually framed as athlete care/performance medicine rather than CQC assessment evidence, regulated activity decisions, provider registration packs, CQC factual accuracy response, board assurance, or CQC-style policy control. Sports governance, safeguarding, and health and safety SportLoMo Governance and Safe Sport, Globocol Safeguarding in Sport, CPOMS StudentSafe for Sports Clubs, Sport:80 Compliance Management Safeguarding, vetting, certification, incident and injury workflows, audit trails, policy acknowledgement, concussion/return-to-play controls, club/federation reporting. Strong on sport compliance, but not a CQC/private healthcare governance platform. Clinical records, medicines, regulated activities, provider registration, CQC evidence categories, and quality-statement assurance would still need a separate layer. Generic healthcare incident/risk/audit systems Radar Healthcare Audit Management, Datix-style incident systems, generic GRC/EHS tools Audits, incidents, risks, action plans, dashboards, evidence trails, safety learning. Often too broad, hospital/social-care oriented, or not athlete-workflow friendly. May not capture sports medical edge cases without extensive configuration. Regulatory and digital-health guardrails CQC evidence categories, ICO special category data guidance, NHS clinical safety DCB0129, MHRA software and AI as a medical device Evidence taxonomy, health data duties, clinical risk management, medical-device boundary. These are not products, but they shape product design, documentation, risk management, and go-to-market limits.

docs.title: Evaluation criteria
docs.text:
The useful comparison is not "which product is best?" but "which product category already solves part of the CQC/Rugby League problem?"

Criterion Why it matters for a professional club CQC evidence mapping The platform must organise evidence under CQC's evidence categories, quality statements, and provider governance expectations. Sports medical workflow The platform must work around fixtures, training, pitch-side incidents, concussion, return-to-play, player loans, and medical confidentiality. RFL Medical Standards coverage The platform must support mandatory staff, EAP, equipment, anti-doping, concussion, cardiac screening, infection control, and record-keeping evidence. Clinical record boundary The product must either store clinical notes safely or integrate with a specialist EMR without weakening evidence traceability. Board assurance Board users need risk, exceptions, overdue evidence, service-model changes, and assurance packs, not raw confidential medical notes. Contractor/provider model Clubs may use external doctors, ambulance providers, diagnostics providers, or screening providers. The platform needs contracts, credentials, scope, and registration/exemption evidence. Data protection and permissions Player health data is special category data. Role-based access, audit trails, disclosure logs, retention, and export controls are core requirements. Clinical safety and medical-device boundary If the system moves from evidence and records into recommendations or decision support, NHS DCB0129 and MHRA software guidance become design constraints.

docs.title: CQC compliance and quality platforms
docs.text:
Agilio TeamNet is a relevant comparator because Agilio positions it for independent healthcare providers as a practice-management platform for CQC compliance, knowledge management, HR, and mandatory training. Its CQC compliance features include compliance information, significant events, fridge checks, staff training, safety alerts, review dates, audit trails, version acknowledgements, and inspection readiness. Agilio also positions its wider primary care offering around CQC compliance, HR, rotas, mandatory training, and information sharing for GP practices, PCNs, federations, and independent providers.

Radar Healthcare is a strong example of the regulated-provider pattern: CQC evidence, audits, action plans, incident/risk management, document control, workforce compliance, and reporting. Vantage presents a similar quality-management and compliance pattern for healthcare providers. QCS is more policy and procedure oriented, with regulated-sector compliance content and update discipline. Log my Care is useful as a social-care digital-record and CQC-readiness reference, but its care-home/home-care workflow is not a sports medical workflow.

The lesson is that CQC-readiness and healthcare-operations products are good at evidence, policies, audits, actions, workforce compliance, mandatory training, and inspection packs. They are weak on sports-specific concepts such as concussion, athlete availability, RFL mandatory equipment, match-day medical cover, loan-player handovers, anti-doping, and the sports-ground exclusion.

docs.title: Sports medicine EMR and athlete management
docs.text:
Kitman Labs Performance Medicine, Teamworks AMS / Smartabase, AthleteMonitoring, SportsWareOnLine, and Orreco Medical Notes show mature patterns for athlete health records, injury and illness tracking, SOAP notes, documents, medical screening, concussion, medications, rehabilitation, dashboards, and performance-data integration.

The lesson is that sports EMRs are likely better than a new CQC product at day-to-day clinical records. The gap is that they do not usually generate CQC registration rationale, board-level CQC assurance, RFL clause evidence packs, CQC factual accuracy responses, or regulated-provider governance documentation.

docs.title: Sports governance, safeguarding, and compliance platforms
docs.text:
SportLoMo, Globocol, CPOMS StudentSafe for Sports Clubs, and Sport:80 show strong sports-governance patterns: safeguarding, welfare, credentials, vetting, certifications, policy acknowledgements, incident workflows, eligibility, concussion/return-to-play controls, dashboards, and federation-level reporting.

The lesson is that sport already has software patterns for governance and compliance. The missing layer is healthcare-provider governance: regulated activity scope, clinical record integrity, confidentiality, medicines, diagnostics, contractor healthcare oversight, and CQC-style evidence.

docs.title: Digital health and regulatory guardrails
docs.text:
The platform must respect CQC's evidence categories, the ICO's special category data rules for health data, NHS DCB0129 if manufacturing health IT with clinical risk, and MHRA software and AI as a medical device guidance if the system starts to make or materially influence clinical decisions.

The lesson is to keep the MVP on evidence, workflow, assurance, reminders, and structured documentation. Clinical decision support should be a deliberate later decision, not an accidental feature.

docs.title: What this suggests
docs.text:
The opportunity is not to rebuild every component from scratch. A practical platform could sit between existing tools:

  • CQC compliance platforms provide useful patterns for policies, audits, action plans, and evidence packs.
  • Sports medicine EMRs provide useful patterns for athlete injury, illness, medicines, rehabilitation, and return-to-play workflows.
  • Sports safeguarding and governance systems provide useful patterns for vetting, eligibility, safe sport, safeguarding, and club/federation reporting.

The distinctive product layer would be the CQC/Rugby League interpretation layer:

  • is the service outside scope, excluded, contractor-led, or potentially registerable?
  • what evidence proves the conclusion?
  • which records support CQC-style assurance?
  • what must be reported to the board?
  • what service change triggers reassessment?

docs.title: Research view: build, buy, or integrate candidates
docs.text:
This table is provisional. It identifies where further research should focus before any build/buy/integrate decision is made.

Need Best route Rationale CQC/RFL interpretation, scope decisions, evidence packs Research likely gap Test with CQC specialists, sports lawyers, and club Heads of Medical before assuming a product gap. Day-to-day clinical notes and athlete EMR Research likely integration area Sports EMRs already do much of this. Need to know what clubs currently use and whether exports/APIs are practical. Workforce credentials and training Research overlap Could sit in club HR, RFL systems, sport governance platforms, or a new evidence layer. Safeguarding and welfare case management Research separation boundary Existing safeguarding tools may already be better. Access and confidentiality boundaries need careful research. Board dashboard and action plan Research likely gap Need to confirm what boards actually need and what medical data they must not see. Medical-device or clinical decision support Research red line Do not treat this as an MVP feature without clinical safety and medical-device analysis.

docs.title: Procurement questions
docs.text:
For any tool being bought or integrated, the club should ask:

  • Can it export records and evidence in a usable format for CQC, RFL, board, insurer, or legal review?
  • Can it separate confidential clinical notes from board-visible assurance?
  • Does it support role-based access, audit trails, retention rules, and disclosure logs?
  • Can evidence be mapped to RFL Medical Standards and CQC quality statements?
  • Can it handle match-day, training-ground, travel, loan-player, and contractor workflows?
  • Does it integrate through API or structured export?
  • Is UK GDPR and special category data handling documented?
  • Does the vendor make any clinical decision-support claims that could create clinical safety or medical-device obligations?

docs.title: Research gap statement
docs.text:
No researched product has yet been shown to combine all of the following in one Rugby League-specific package:

  • CQC registration boundary decision support for sports medical services;
  • RFL Medical Standards clause evidence mapping;
  • board assurance packs that avoid inappropriate disclosure of player clinical data;
  • integration-ready links to athlete EMRs and sports governance platforms;
  • contractor/provider due diligence for doctors, diagnostics, cardiac screening, ambulance cover, and clinic services;
  • CQC factual accuracy and assessment-response packs.

This is a research gap, not yet proof of a market opportunity. The next evidence should come from club interviews, procurement review, and testing real current workflows.

docs.title: Research still needed
docs.text:
• Identify current medical record, safeguarding, HR, incident, and governance tools used by Rugby League and comparable football/rugby union clubs.
  • Assess Agilio TeamNet specifically as a healthcare-operations comparator for policies, significant events, safety alerts, workforce training, acknowledgements, and CQC inspection readiness.
  • Check whether sports EMRs can export the evidence needed for Minimum Compliance Pack Research.
  • Check whether CQC compliance platforms can be configured around sports-ground exclusions and RFL standards.
  • Compare vendor claims against ICO, clinical safety, and medical-device guardrails.
  • Identify where clubs would prefer documentation, consultancy, software, or a hybrid service.

docs.title: Why private healthcare is the right comparator
docs.text:
CQC defines independent healthcare as healthcare provided by organisations that are not NHS trusts or NHS GP services. This includes private companies, charities, social enterprises, voluntary and faith-based organisations, and individual providers of care.

A professional Rugby League club with a medical department is not a typical private clinic, but the comparator becomes strong where the club:

  • provides care outside the immediate participant/event context;
  • charges for services;
  • uses clinical rooms as a treatment site;
  • employs or contracts regulated clinicians;
  • controls diagnostics, records, medicines, referrals, or treatment pathways;
  • treats people who are not contracted players.

docs.title: Provider-level accountability
docs.text:
Independent healthcare providers need clear legal-entity accountability. A club should mirror this by naming:

  • the legal entity that owns the medical service;
  • the board member or executive accountable for clinical quality;
  • the clinical governance lead;
  • the registered manager or equivalent day-to-day clinical manager where CQC registration applies;
  • the nominated individual where the provider is an organisation applying for registration.

docs.title: Statement of purpose discipline
docs.text:
CQC registration applications require a clear description of services, regulated activities, locations, and service types. Even outside registration, a club should maintain a medical service statement of purpose that defines:

  • who the service is for;
  • what services are and are not provided;
  • locations and mobile/event arrangements;
  • clinical opening hours and emergency cover;
  • referral routes;
  • exclusion boundaries, especially no-public-clinic or no-third-party-treatment rules if relying on sports exclusions.

docs.title: Governance evidence pack
docs.text:
Independent healthcare providers are expected to show systems before registration and during inspection. CQC's new provider guidance is useful as a checklist even where a club concludes registration is not required. A club can reuse the same evidence structure:

  • policies: consent, records, medicines, safeguarding, chaperones, infection prevention, complaints, duty of candour, incident reporting, data protection, business continuity;
  • people: professional registration, indemnity, DBS where needed, training, supervision, appraisal, scope of practice;
  • environment: premises risk assessment, equipment inventory, calibration, cleaning, emergency kit checks;
  • care: clinical records, care plans, return-to-play decisions, referral letters, discharge notes;
  • assurance: audits, incidents, complaints, outcomes, player feedback, medical committee minutes, board reports.

docs.title: Risk and performance information
docs.text:
CQC's well-led governance quality statement expects leaders to use information about risk, performance, and outcomes. For a club, useful indicators include:

  • serious injuries, head injury assessments, concussions, failed return-to-play steps, and recurrent injuries;
  • emergency transfers and ambulance activations;
  • medicines incidents and controlled-drug discrepancies where applicable;
  • safeguarding referrals and welfare concerns;
  • equipment failures or missing checks;
  • clinical record completion and audit compliance;
  • clinician credential expiry;
  • complaints, concerns, whistleblowing, and duty-of-candour reviews;
  • service pressure, match congestion, travel, and staffing gaps.

docs.title: Registration boundary discipline
docs.text: Independent healthcare providers define their regulated activities, locations, service users, and statement of purpose before operating. A club should borrow that discipline even where its core player-only service is excluded from CQC registration. The biggest private-healthcare synergy is to require a new scope assessment before adding public access, diagnostics, injections, IV services, prescribing pathways, remote triage, or paid clinic services.

docs.title: Contracting synergies
docs.text:
Independent healthcare governance is especially useful when the club outsources. Contracts should specify:

  • which entity directs and controls the regulated activity;
  • whether the contractor is CQC registered, exempt, or outside scope, and why;
  • clinician qualifications, professional registration, indemnity, DBS, and CPD requirements;
  • records ownership, access, retention, and subject access handling;
  • incident reporting, safeguarding, complaints, duty of candour, and notification responsibilities;
  • medicines and equipment responsibilities;
  • audit rights and quality reporting cadence;
  • escalation when sporting performance objectives conflict with clinical judgement.

docs.title: Cultural synergy
docs.text:
The private healthcare model creates a helpful separation between clinical judgement and commercial or sporting pressure. A club should make explicit that:

  • clinicians owe professional duties to the person receiving care;
  • return-to-play decisions sit with appropriately qualified clinicians;
  • coaches and executives may receive fitness information only within consent, confidentiality, and data protection rules;
  • players can raise concerns without selection or contract retaliation;
  • medical governance reports to the board, not solely through performance staff.

docs.title: Product Research Roadmap
docs.text: This page records the next research tasks for a CQC inspection preparation platform for professional sports clubs, using Rugby League as the starting example. It is intentionally research-first: the wiki should establish what clubs, clinicians, boards, regulators, and league rules require before treating anything as a product specification.

docs.title: Current checklist
docs.text: Status Item Output in the wiki Notes Scope decision recorded 1. Define research audience and decision context Research Scope and Assumptions and Scope Decision Record Current audience: online CQC inspection preparation platform for professional sports clubs, using a Championship Rugby League Club as the starting example. Scenario matrix added 2. Define the minimum viable compliance pack Minimum Compliance Pack Research and Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix Research inventory tested against six scenarios. Still needs external validation before final pack design. Status register added 3. Map RFL Medical Standards clause-by-clause RFL Clause Evidence Map and RFL Clause Status Register Status, Championship relevance, record visibility, and validation gaps added. Still needs RFL/Head of Medical validation. Deferred 4. Design the platform information architecture Product blueprint update Should wait until items 2, 3, 5, and 6 are better understood. Question bank added 5. Research CQC scope decision workflow CQC Scope Decision Research and CQC Scope Question Bank Question bank added and tested against the six scenarios. Still needs regulatory validation. Capability comparison added 6. Deepen market and tool research Platform Tool Landscape and Tool Capability Comparison Capability comparison and vendor verification questions added. Still needs vendor/current-system validation.

docs.title: Working priorities
docs.text:
The current priority is to use the scope decision in Scope Decision Record to reshape the research into a guided preparation journey for a professional sports club new to CQC, using a Championship Rugby League Club as the starting example.

The research should answer five practical questions:

  1. Who is the research for, and what decision should it support?
  2. What evidence would a club reasonably need to hold?
  3. Which RFL requirements are mandatory, best practice, information-only, or competition-specific?
  4. What facts determine the CQC scope decision?
  5. Which existing tools solve parts of the problem, and what gaps remain?

docs.title: 1. Research audience and decision context
docs.text:
This item is now reframed in Research Scope and Assumptions and provisionally decided in Scope Decision Record. The research should not start with a commercial product buying group. It should support:

  • professional sports clubs as the platform audience, with a Championship Rugby League Club as the starting example;
  • board and Head of Medical understanding;
  • preparation for CQC-style scrutiny where this is new to the organisation;
  • translation of existing medical/clinical governance into inspection-grade evidence;
  • future platform research without jumping to software specification.

docs.title: 2. Minimum viable compliance pack
docs.text:
The pack research should identify which documents and records are genuinely necessary before deciding what to build:

  • CQC scope decision record;
  • medical service description;
  • RFL standards evidence dashboard;
  • clinical governance meeting record;
  • contractor due diligence file;
  • incident, serious injury, concussion, medicines, and complaint records;
  • annual board assurance report.

Current output: Minimum Compliance Pack Research and Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix.

docs.title: 3. RFL clause-by-clause map
docs.text:
The first-pass detailed map is in RFL Clause Evidence Map, with status and visibility detail in RFL Clause Status Register. It should be treated as a research baseline, not a final control library. It still needs:

  • exact mandatory versus best-practice status;
  • competition-specific differences;
  • evidence owner;
  • evidence cadence;
  • platform fields and export format.

docs.title: 4. Platform information architecture
docs.text:
The existing Platform Product Blueprint should be expanded into data objects and permissions:

  • club, venue, service, provider, clinician, fixture, training session, player, incident, record, audit, action, evidence item, policy, board report;
  • role-based access for medical confidentiality, board assurance, safeguarding, and external contractors;
  • exports for CQC, RFL, board, insurer, and clinical audit use.

docs.title: 5. CQC scope decision research
docs.text:
The Registration Decision Model should be expanded into a decision research protocol before it becomes any software workflow:

  • identify activity;
  • identify provider/control model;
  • test sports-ground and temporary sporting-event exclusions;
  • test diagnostics, remote advice, ambulance transport, medicines, and clinic triggers;
  • capture evidence and legal/clinical review points;
  • trigger reassessment when the service model changes.

Current output: CQC Scope Decision Research and CQC Scope Question Bank.

docs.title: 6. Market and tool research
docs.text:
The expanded Platform Tool Landscape should be used to understand adjacent markets before deciding whether to build, buy, or integrate. Current research should focus on:

  • which capabilities already exist in CQC compliance tools;
  • which capabilities already exist in sports EMRs;
  • which capabilities already exist in sports governance/safeguarding systems;
  • which product claims create clinical safety, information governance, or medical-device risk;
  • what evidence clubs would need to procure or integrate any tool safely.

Current output: Platform Tool Landscape and Tool Capability Comparison.

docs.title: Registration Decision Model
docs.text:
Use this model before launching, changing, contracting, or commercialising any club medical service. It operationalises CQC's scope of registration and should be read with CQC Governance Baseline.

For difficult fact patterns, use this page together with CQC Registration Edge Cases.

docs.title: Step 1: Describe the service
docs.text:
Record:

  • service name and owner;
  • patient/user group;
  • premises and mobile/event locations;
  • clinicians and professional roles;
  • activities performed;
  • medicines, diagnostics, procedures, records, referrals, and follow-up;
  • whether anyone pays for the service directly or indirectly;
  • whether the service is solely for players, participants, or event attendees.

docs.title: Step 2: Identify possible regulated activities
docs.text:
Check whether the service includes:

  • treatment of disease, disorder, or injury;
  • diagnostic and screening procedures;
  • surgical procedures;
  • transport, triage, or medical advice provided remotely;
  • maternity or midwifery services;
  • nursing care or personal care in its own right.

Most Rugby League medical services will start with treatment of disease, disorder, or injury, but diagnostics and screening are common expansion points.

docs.title: Step 3: Test exclusions and exemptions
docs.text:
For sport, document whether the activity is:

  • first aid in an unexpected or dangerous situation requiring immediate action;
  • first aid by trained non-healthcare professionals or a first-aid organisation;
  • treatment at a sports ground, gymnasium, or associated premises;
  • solely for people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events;
  • temporary provision for a sporting event.

If all relevant conditions are met, record the rationale and evidence. If the service also treats the public or provides wider clinic services, do not assume the sports exclusion applies.

docs.title: Step 4: Identify who carries on the activity
docs.text:
Ask who controls:

  • clinical policy;
  • clinician appointment and supervision;
  • patient eligibility;
  • records system;
  • medicines and equipment;
  • complaints and incidents;
  • data controller decisions;
  • referral pathways;
  • payment and service terms.

If the club controls these features, the club may be the provider for CQC purposes. If a contractor controls them, check the contractor's registration or exemption. If control is shared, clarify it before service launch using CQC's legal entity/provider guidance and the contractor due diligence checklist.

docs.title: Step 5: Determine locations
docs.text:
Map whether the activity is carried on:

  • at a stadium medical room;
  • at a training ground;
  • at an academy site;
  • from a mobile unit;
  • remotely from an administrative base;
  • from a third-party clinic;
  • at away fixtures or temporary events.

CQC location rules and location conditions can matter even where the legal entity is clear.

docs.title: Step 6: Decide the governance route
docs.text: Outcome Action Clearly outside CQC scope Keep documented rationale and maintain sport/clinical governance. Excluded because sports-ground/event conditions apply Keep evidence that the service is solely for participants/attendees and not a public clinic. Contractor is responsible and registered Verify registration, contract clinical governance, and receive quality reports. Contractor is responsible and says exempt Require written exemption rationale and review annually. Club likely carries on regulated activity Obtain legal/CQC advice, identify activities, locations, nominated individual, registered manager, and evidence pack. Borderline or mixed model Pause launch or ring-fence service until control, users, locations, and activities are clear.

docs.title: Minimum documentation pack
docs.text:
• regulated activity analysis;
  • sports exclusion analysis;
  • service statement of purpose;
  • clinical governance structure;
  • policies and SOPs;
  • contracts and service-level agreements;
  • clinician credential register;
  • premises and equipment register;
  • incident, complaint, and safeguarding process;
  • records and data protection process;
  • audit plan and board reporting template.

See Assurance Pack Templates for working templates.

For the research work needed before any digital implementation, see CQC Scope Decision Research. For a later digital implementation concept, see Platform Product Blueprint.

  • Worked Examples shows the model applied to common Rugby League club scenarios.
  • Private Healthcare Synergies explains how independent healthcare providers structure the same registration discipline.
  • Governance Operating Model describes how to keep the assessment live after launch.
  • CQC Scope Decision Research records the facts and evidence that must be researched before a decision tool is designed.
  • Platform Product Blueprint shows how this model can become a product workflow with evidence mapping, reminders, and board reporting.

docs.title: Research Scope and Assumptions
docs.text:
This page reframes roadmap item 1. The immediate issue is not "target users and buying groups"; that is too product-shaped. The research first needs to define audience, decision context, and boundary.

The working question is: what is this research trying to make clear, for whom, and for what decision?

The current scope decision is recorded in Scope Decision Record.

docs.title: Scope options
docs.text: Option Description What it would prioritise Risk if chosen too early Single-club governance research Research focused on one professional Rugby League club, such as a Championship club medical service. Board briefing, Head of Medical pack, RFL evidence, CQC scope decision, contractor controls. May not generalise to other clubs or sports. Professional Rugby League framework Research reusable across Championship, Super League, Women's Super League, academy, reserves, and other professional Rugby League contexts. Competition-specific RFL evidence, club operating model, league reporting, common scenarios. May become too broad before one club workflow is understood. Professional sport healthcare governance Research across Rugby League and comparable professional sports. Sports-ground exclusions, private healthcare edge cases, sport EMRs, safeguarding, medical governance patterns. May dilute the Rugby League/RFL specificity. Platform opportunity research Research a possible online product or service. Tool landscape, evidence packs, workflow, exports, integrations, vendor gaps. Risks jumping to solution before requirements are validated. Board assurance pack Research the immediate board-facing output from Head of Medical. Plain-English board paper, risk register, evidence pack, decision record, action plan. May under-research operational and clinical record detail.

docs.title: Current working boundary
docs.text:
Until changed, the wiki should assume this order:

  1. Primary scope: an online CQC inspection preparation platform for professional sports clubs, using a Championship Rugby League Club as the starting example.
  2. Immediate output: a guided preparation and assurance knowledge base for board, Head of Medical, clinical, operational, safeguarding/welfare, and contractor-facing understanding.
  3. Secondary output: reusable research structure that may later support other professional Rugby League clubs and comparable professional sports clubs.
  4. Product/platform angle: relevant as an online preparation experience, but not yet a full software implementation specification.
  5. Out of scope for now: full product specification, pricing, sales personas, buying committee design, implementation architecture, and vendor selection.

docs.title: Audience and decision context
docs.text: Audience Decision they may need to make Research they need Board/CEO Is the club exposed to CQC registration or healthcare governance risk? What needs action? Board briefing, scope conclusion, risk register, assurance pack, unresolved issues. Head of Medical What evidence is needed to show the service is safe, governed, and RFL/CQC-aware? Minimum pack, RFL evidence map, clinical governance model, contractor controls. Club operations Are venues, EAPs, contractors, equipment, and match-day processes evidenced? Scenario matrix, EAP/equipment/contractor evidence, command-and-control. Clinicians What records, professional boundaries, and escalation routes protect clinical judgement and player welfare? Clinical record boundary, medicines/concussion controls, information-sharing rules. Safeguarding/welfare What overlaps with academy, mental health, disclosure, welfare, and child protection? Safeguarding and clinical-record separation, welfare escalation, access rules. Legal/CQC adviser Does the research identify the facts needed for a defensible CQC scope view? CQC scope question bank, worked examples, contracts, service descriptions. RFL/league source Are RFL standards interpreted correctly for the competition context? RFL clause status register and Championship-specific uncertainties. Vendors Can existing tools support the evidence needs? Tool capability comparison and export/permission questions.

docs.title: Scope decisions still needed
docs.text:
Before moving into validation interviews or solution design, these questions need answering:

Question Why it matters Is the concrete reference club Sheffield Eagles, a generic Championship club, Rugby League generally, or a comparable professional sport? Determines how specific the pack, governing-body references, and RFL status register should be. Is the primary deliverable a board paper, a guided online preparation pathway, or both? Determines whether pages should optimise for directors, operational medical teams, or first-time CQC learning. Should academy/scholarship care be in the first research phase? Adds safeguarding, parent/guardian, school, and child welfare complexity. Should the research cover public clinics and paid treatment only as risk triggers, or as possible future services? Changes the depth of CQC registration research needed. Should platform research remain secondary until after governance scope is validated? Prevents premature product framing.

docs.title: Recommended next posture
docs.text:
The safest next posture is:

  • treat item 1 as research audience and decision context, with Scope Decision Record as the current answer;
  • keep item 2 as the evidence pack research;
  • keep item 3 as RFL status and evidence research;
  • keep item 5 as CQC factual decision research;
  • keep item 6 as market/tool research only;
  • defer item 4 platform information architecture until the scope questions above are settled.

docs.title: Research Validation Plan
docs.text:
This page records what must be validated before the wiki should move from research into solution design.

The aim is to prevent the project from becoming overconfident. Each workstream should identify who can confirm or challenge the assumption, what evidence to ask for, and what would change the research conclusion.

docs.title: Validation groups
docs.text: Group What to validate Evidence to request Output Head of Medical Whether the minimum pack, RFL clause map, concussion controls, medicines controls, and contractor evidence match real club workflow. Current medical governance calendar, anonymised examples of records/checklists, pain points, current tools. Validated or revised core pack and RFL evidence map. Club board/CEO What assurance directors need and what clinical detail they should not see. Board paper examples, risk appetite, committee structure, decision thresholds. Board-visible evidence boundary. Club operations/match manager Venue, EAP, medical-room, equipment, ambulance access, contractor command-and-control, and fixture logistics. EAP, venue checklist, match-day briefing, contractor plan, venue-change process. Operational evidence checklist. Safeguarding/welfare lead Academy, under-18, welfare, mental health, disclosure, and information-sharing boundaries. Safeguarding policy, welfare escalation route, academy consent model, case-access rules. Safeguarding/clinical record separation. RFL or league medical source Competition-specific requirements, mandatory/best-practice status, reporting deadlines, and evidence expectations. Current RFL Medical Standards clarification, competition table confirmation, reporting templates. Hardened Championship-specific register. CQC/regulatory lawyer CQC scope decision logic, sports exclusions, contractor/control, diagnostics, remote advice, public clinics, and registration risk. Scenario matrix, question bank, service descriptions, contracts. Validated scope decision research and stop/advice triggers. Vendors Whether existing systems can export the evidence required and enforce permission boundaries. Demo using scenario evidence, export samples, API docs, access-control model, audit logs. Tool capability comparison evidence. Information governance adviser Health data, special category data, controller/processor roles, board reporting, retention, subject access, and sharing. Data flows, privacy notices, contracts, access matrix, retention schedule. IG risk register and access model.

docs.title: Validation questions by item
docs.text: Roadmap item Validation questions Item 2: Minimum compliance pack Which components are truly minimum? Which are scenario appendices? Which evidence is board-visible? Which evidence must remain clinical/restricted? Item 3: RFL clause map Which clauses are mandatory, best practice, information-only, or competition-specific for Championship? What evidence would RFL actually expect? Item 5: CQC scope question bank Which questions are legally decisive? Which facts require stop-and-seek-advice? Which outcomes should never be automated? Item 6: Tool landscape Which current tools are actually used by clubs? Which can export usable evidence? Which create clinical safety, IG, or medical-device concerns?

docs.title: Validation artefacts to collect
docs.text:
• Anonymised current club medical governance pack.
  • Current fixture/training cover rota and medical staff evidence list.
  • Example EAP and pre-match briefing record.
  • Example contractor contract or service specification for cardiac screening or event medical cover.
  • Example concussion pathway evidence pack.
  • Example board medical assurance report or board risk paper.
  • Vendor export sample for athlete clinical records and compliance evidence.
  • CQC/legal review note on the six scenario matrix cases.

docs.title: Completion criteria before solution design
docs.text:
Research should not move into solution design until:

  • the six scenario matrix cases have been validated by at least one Head of Medical or equivalent;
  • the RFL status register has been checked for Championship-specific requirements;
  • the CQC question bank has been reviewed by a regulatory specialist;
  • the board-visible/restricted-clinical evidence boundary has been agreed;
  • at least two vendors or current club systems have been tested against the evidence export questions;
  • unresolved assumptions are listed openly on the relevant page.
