docs.title: RFL Clause Evidence Map
docs.text:
This page is a first-pass research map from the RFL Medical Standards 2026 into CQC-style evidence. It sits beneath the higher-level RFL Medical Standards Map. For status classification, Championship relevance, and board-visible versus restricted evidence, use RFL Clause Status Register.

The purpose is not to reproduce the RFL standards or to finalise software requirements. It is to identify what a club may need to evidence, and what still needs validation, if asked, "How do you know this medical service is governed?"

docs.title: Research maturity
docs.text: Area Current maturity What still needs doing Clause coverage First-pass A1-F11 coverage is in place. Check every clause against the RFL PDF and add missing subclauses where they materially change evidence. Mandatory status Mandatory language is clear for many areas, but not all rows are yet classified. Add a mandatory / best practice / information-only / competition-specific status column after validation. Competition specificity Championship is referenced where obvious, for example cardiac screening and equipment. Confirm differences for Super League, Championship, WSL, academy, reserves, scholarship, community, and away/travel contexts. Evidence ownership Indicative owners are listed. Validate with a Head of Medical, club operations lead, welfare/safeguarding lead, and external provider. CQC mapping High-level CQC lens is included. Cross-check against CQC evidence categories and quality statements once the evidence pack is stabilised.

docs.title: Status triage to validate
docs.text: RFL area Apparent status from source Validation note A1 medical staff registration Mandatory. Confirm role-by-role requirement and club evidence route. A3 prehospital trauma courses Mandatory for field-of-play personnel. Confirm acceptable equivalents, grace periods, and competition differences. A4 attendance at matches and training Mandatory. Confirm exact cover requirement for Championship home, away, training, academy, and reserve contexts. A5 match-day rules and responsibilities Mandatory in substance, with competition-specific tables. Extract competition-specific minimum medical staff table into a separate research note if needed. B1 medical note keeping and injury audit Mandatory. Confirm required record content, retention, system expectations, and audit reporting. B4 serious injury/death reporting Mandatory. Align with club incident, board, insurer, safeguarding, and possible CQC notification analysis. B5 medical emergency action plan Mandatory. Confirm evidence needed for each venue and temporary venue change. C1 mandatory medical equipment and drugs box Mandatory. Confirm home/away responsibilities and evidence of pre-match checks. C2 equipment list Mixed mandatory and best practice. Separate minimum mandatory equipment from best-practice equipment. D anti-doping Mandatory elements and information-only special topics. Separate checking obligations from guidance and education material. E concussion Mandatory regulations. Confirm reporting deadlines, professional-game scope, community-game boundary, and loan-player notification. F cardiac screening Mandatory for specified competitions including Championship. Confirm provider, registration/exemption, abnormal result, and clearance evidence. F mental health and other policies Mixed mandatory, best-practice, and information-only content. Classify each subsection and identify which controls are medical, welfare, safeguarding, or HR-led.

docs.title: How to read the map
docs.text: Field Meaning RFL area Clause or clause group in the 2026 standards. Evidence to hold Practical records a club should retain or generate. Primary owner Usual accountable role. Cadence When the evidence should be checked. CQC lens Closest CQC-style assurance domain. Platform implication What an online platform should capture or automate.

docs.title: Section A: Medical personnel
docs.text: RFL area Evidence to hold Primary owner Cadence CQC lens Platform implication A1: Medical staff registration with the RFL RFL registration status, professional registration, role, scope, indemnity, DBS where relevant, expiry dates. Head of Medical Pre-season and before appointment Safe; well-led Workforce register with expiry alerts and evidence uploads. A2: Definition of medical staff terms Role descriptions for doctor, physiotherapist, paramedic, sports rehabilitator, sports therapist, first aider, and locum/second clinician. Head of Medical Pre-season and onboarding Safe; effective Role templates mapped to permitted duties and escalation limits. A3: Prehospital trauma courses IMMOFP/iIMMOFP or accepted equivalent, course dates, recertification, evidence of practical competence. Head of Medical Pre-season and expiry Safe; effective Credential matrix with blocked deployment if mandatory training lapses. A4: Medical staff attendance at matches and training Match and training cover rota, acceptance of fixture cover, absences, substitutions, locum induction. Head of Medical and operations Every fixture and training block Safe; responsive Cover planner linked to fixture and training calendars. A5a-f: Match-day medical roles and responsibilities Named medical team, home/away responsibilities, communications equipment checks, pitch-side treatment rules, second clinician arrangements, competition-specific minimum cover. Match-day doctor or lead clinician Every fixture Safe; well-led Match-day checklist with role assignment, confirmations, and exception log. A6: Urgent medical cover situations Contingency plan for loss of doctor, paramedic, ambulance, equipment, communications, or venue access. Head of Medical and match operations Every fixture and incident review Safe; responsive Contingency workflow with "cannot proceed" decision points. A7: Medical duties Accountability for mandatory equipment, venue readiness, medical decisions, documentation, and player welfare. Match-day lead clinician Every fixture Safe; effective; caring Duty checklist and after-action confirmation. A8: Medical treatment for teams travelling to France Travel medical plan, insurance, records, medicine controls, French provider arrangements, legal/indemnity review. Head of Medical and operations Before relevant travel Safe; well-led Travel service-risk checklist and document pack.

docs.title: Section B: Ethics, governance, planning, information and data
docs.text: RFL area Evidence to hold Primary owner Cadence CQC lens Platform implication B1: Medical note keeping, RFL research, injury audit Clinical record policy, named record system, record completeness audit, injury audit submissions, research/data-sharing approvals. Head of Medical Continuous; quarterly audit Safe; effective; well-led Secure records/evidence index with completeness dashboard. B2: Sharing information Consent/confidentiality policy, player information sheet, disclosure log, role-based access model. Head of Medical and data protection lead Pre-season and each disclosure Caring; well-led Information-sharing workflow with reason, recipient, consent/legal basis, and audit trail. B3: Loan-player information sharing Loan medical handover record, parent-club notification, injury/concussion/medicine status, access limits. Head of Medical Each loan movement and incident Safe; responsive Loan-player workflow with mandatory handover fields. B4: Reporting death or serious injury Serious incident protocol, notification record, incident chronology, board notification, learning review. Head of Medical and chief executive Each incident Safe; well-led Serious incident case file and notification checklist. B5: Medical emergency action plan Site-specific EAP for stadium and training venues, ambulance access, emergency department details, communications route, next-of-kin route, review date. Head of Medical and operations Pre-season, venue change, match change Safe; responsive Venue EAP module with version control and sharing log. B6: Pre-match briefing by home club Briefing attendance, agenda, emergency roles, equipment confirmation, ambulance access, opposition sign-off. Home club lead clinician Every home fixture Safe; well-led Digital pre-match briefing form with opposition acknowledgement. B7a-e: On-field processes Green card, blood bin, kick-off, 18th player, injured-player process records and post-match exception review. Match-day lead clinician Every fixture Safe; effective Fixture process checklist and exception reporting.

docs.title: Section C: Equipment and facilities
docs.text: RFL area Evidence to hold Primary owner Cadence CQC lens Platform implication C1: Mandatory medical equipment and mandatory drugs box Equipment inventory, drug box list, expiry dates, oxygen/AED checks, missing item escalation. Head of Medical Before fixtures/training and monthly Safe Equipment register with barcode/photo evidence and expiry alerts. C2: Full minimum equipment and best practice list Competition-specific equipment checklist, best-practice gap log, action plan. Head of Medical Pre-season and quarterly Safe; effective Checklist library by competition and venue type. C3: Clinical waste disposal Waste contract, sharps process, collection records, spill process, incident log. Head of Medical and operations Monthly and after incidents Safe Waste compliance record and incident linkage. C4: Treatment-room facility standards Room audit, stretcher access, handwashing, cleanable surfaces, privacy, internet access for notes, clinical waste, infection prevention. Head of Medical and venue operations Pre-season, venue change, quarterly Safe; caring Venue facility audit with photo evidence and action tracking.

docs.title: Section D: Anti-doping
docs.text: RFL area Evidence to hold Primary owner Cadence CQC lens Platform implication D1: Anti-doping Anti-doping policy, education records, player/support-person acknowledgements, UKAD/RFL communication records. Head of Medical and anti-doping lead Pre-season and updates Safe; well-led Policy acknowledgement and training register. D2: Checking medication and supplements Medication check record, Global DRO / UKAD Search Check Apply evidence, supplement risk discussion, prescribing notes. Prescriber or lead clinician Each medicine/supplement decision Safe; effective Medicine check workflow linked to player and clinician. D3: Prohibited substances and TUE TUE decision record, proactive/retroactive TUE status, supporting clinical evidence, submission and outcome dates. Club doctor Each relevant treatment Safe; well-led TUE tracker with deadline and document controls. D3a-c: IV infusions and TUE routes IV indication, volume, setting, clinician, TUE assessment, emergency rationale if applicable. Club doctor Each IV use Safe; effective High-risk medicine/procedure form with escalation. D4-D5: Special topics and beta-2 agonists Inhaler medicine checks, diagnosis evidence, dose advice, player education, exception review. Club doctor Each relevant prescription/review Safe Condition-specific medicine checklist.

docs.title: Section E: Concussion and head injuries
docs.text: RFL area Evidence to hold Primary owner Cadence CQC lens Platform implication E1: Purpose, scope and interpretation Club concussion policy, RFL guidance acknowledgement, player/staff education. Head of Medical Pre-season and rule change Safe; well-led Policy versioning and training acknowledgement. E2: Pre-season baselines and assessments Baseline register, Cognigram status, SCAT6 status, unavailable/invalid baseline plan. Head of Medical Before contact training and season Safe; effective Baseline dashboard with "not cleared for contact" status. E2.1-E2.5: Cognigram, SCAT6 and neurological assessments Test reports, invalid baseline exception, additional assessment rationale. Club doctor or delegated clinician Pre-season and post-injury Safe; effective Assessment record store linked to clearance workflow. E3: Recognise and remove Removal record, signs/symptoms, pitch-side decision, player refusal/escalation if relevant. Match/training clinician Each suspected head injury Safe; caring Incident form with mandatory "removed/not returned" fields. E3.2: Pitch-side replay Availability and use of replay, limitation where unavailable, decision note. Match-day doctor Each relevant fixture Safe Replay availability field in fixture setup. E4: HIA process HIA form, time removed, assessment outcome, structural head injury screen, interchange notifications. Match-day doctor Each HIA Safe; effective HIA workflow with mandatory timestamps and upload/export. E5: Post-match and training 3-48 hour aftercare advice, serial evaluations, welfare checks, transport/driving advice. Club doctor or delegated clinician Each concussion/suspected concussion Caring; responsive Follow-up schedule and player advice confirmation. E6: Graduated return to play GRTP stage record, symptom checks, doctor oversight, failed-stage action, final clearance. Club doctor Each concussion Safe; effective GRTP workflow with stage gates and clearance lock. E7: Doctor concussion reporting RFL concussion report, 24-hour submission evidence, parent-club notification, return-to-play form, post-injury Cognigram, specialist reports. Club doctor Each concussion Safe; well-led Deadline tracker and export pack for RFL/club board. E8: SCOAT SCOAT use, findings, follow-up plan, specialist referral where required. Club doctor Each relevant office assessment Effective Assessment upload and referral tracker.

docs.title: Section F: RFL medical policies
docs.text: RFL area Evidence to hold Primary owner Cadence CQC lens Platform implication F1-F2: Blood-borne infectious diseases BBV policy, bleeding injury process, hepatitis B screening/vaccination status where relevant, contaminated equipment process. Head of Medical Pre-season and incidents Safe BBV checklist linked to infection-control and player records. F3: Cardiac screening Screening plan, provider contract, provider registration/exemption rationale, attendance, abnormal result pathway, clearance and communication records. Head of Medical and club doctor Annually where mandatory Safe; effective; well-led Screening workflow with provider due diligence and abnormal-result action tracking. F3.1-F3.4: Cardiac screening general, requirements, frequency, providers Questionnaire, physical exam, ECG/echo or provider-defined results as applicable, provider credentials, annual completion status. Club doctor Annually and on player onboarding Safe; effective Clinical screening register and participation gate. F4: Turning players over on field of play Staff training, spinal injury protocol, scenario practice, incident review. Head of Medical Pre-season and after incident Safe Training record and simulation log. F5: Protective and other equipment Advice records on head guards, mouth guards, boxes, padding, player acknowledgement. Clinician or welfare lead Pre-season and as needed Safe; caring Player education and advice log. F6-F8: Mental health counselling, mental health training, insomnia Counselling route, crisis pathway, mental health training record, insomnia advice/escalation pathway. Head of Medical and welfare lead Pre-season and case-by-case Caring; responsive Welfare referral directory and training dashboard. F9: Social and non-prescribed prescription drugs policy Policy acknowledgement, concern record, case review, RFL escalation where required, confidential support route. Head of Medical and welfare lead Pre-season and each case Safe; caring; well-led Protected case-management workflow with restricted access. F10: Loan protocols Loan handover, fitness status, injury/concussion/medicine disclosure controls, parent-club updates. Head of Medical Each loan movement Safe; responsive Loan-player transfer checklist. F11: Infection control policy Infection control policy, audit, cleaning records, outbreak/escalation process, PPE and hand hygiene evidence. Head of Medical and operations Quarterly and incidents Safe Infection-control audit and action tracker.

docs.title: Evidence pack generated from the clause map
docs.text:
An online platform should be able to generate these packs from the above records:

  • Board assurance pack: compliance dashboard, overdue risks, incidents, action plan, and service-model changes.
  • RFL assurance pack: medical staff, equipment, EAP, concussion, cardiac screening, anti-doping, and mandatory policy evidence.
  • CQC scope pack: service description, provider/control model, sports exclusion rationale, diagnostics/clinic/remote advice checks, and evidence that governance exists even where registration is not required.
  • Incident pack: serious injury/death chronology, notifications, clinical records, debrief, learning, duty-of-candour analysis where relevant.
  • Player transfer/loan pack: agreed medical handover, confidentiality basis, current restrictions, concussion status, and medicines/TUE status.

docs.title: Gaps to resolve before solution design
docs.text:
• Confirm competition-specific variations for Championship, Super League, Women's Super League, academy, reserves, and community contexts.
  • Confirm which RFL evidence is mandatory, best practice, or information only for the specific club.
  • Decide whether the platform stores clinical notes directly or references a specialist sports EMR.
  • Define board-visible summaries that avoid inappropriate disclosure of confidential player health data.
  • Define CQC/legal review points for diagnostics, screening, prescribing, remote advice, ambulance transport, and public/private clinics.
  • Validate the map with someone who has operational responsibility for RFL medical compliance.
  • Link each evidence type to the Minimum Compliance Pack Research inventory before converting it into templates or software fields.

docs.title: RFL Clause Status Register
docs.text:
This page hardens item 3 by separating RFL status, Championship relevance, record visibility, and validation gaps. It should be read with RFL Clause Evidence Map.

The register is based on the RFL Medical Standards 2026. It is a research register, not a substitute for the RFL source document.

docs.title: Status definitions
docs.text: Status Meaning for this research Mandatory The RFL standards expressly identify the area as mandatory or mandatory in substance. Competition-specific mandatory Mandatory for specified competitions, teams, roles, or circumstances. Best practice The RFL standards present the area as best practice or recommended. Information only The RFL standards present the area as information or guidance rather than a direct action requirement. Mixed The section contains mandatory and non-mandatory elements that need separating before final evidence design. Needs validation The source status is not yet sufficiently classified for a club-specific pack.

docs.title: Status register
docs.text: RFL area Research status Championship relevance Club-held evidence Clinician-only or restricted evidence Validation gap A1: Medical staff registration Mandatory Relevant to club medical staff and match/training cover. RFL registration status, professional registration, indemnity, role, expiry dates. Professional fitness issues, occupational health details. Confirm role-by-role evidence route and whether contractor staff are evidenced by club or provider. A2: Medical staff terms Mixed Relevant because role definitions drive cover and delegation. Role descriptions, scope matrix, cover rota. Individual supervision notes where sensitive. Confirm which roles are allowed for Championship home, away, training, academy, and reserves. A3: Prehospital trauma courses Competition-specific mandatory Relevant for field-of-play cover and IMMOFP/iIMMOFP expectations. Course certificates, expiry dates, accepted equivalent record. Individual training remediation. Confirm accepted equivalents and grace periods. A4: Medical staff attendance Mandatory Relevant for matches and training. Fixture/training cover plan, locum induction, attendance exceptions. Individual sickness/HR records. Extract exact Championship cover requirements from the RFL competition table. A5: Match-day rules and responsibilities Mixed mandatory and best practice Highly relevant to home and away Championship fixtures. Match-day medical plan, pre-match confirmations, communication checks, equipment checks, exception log. Clinical decision notes and player treatment records. Separate A5a-f by competition and home/away duty. A6: Urgent medical cover situations Information only Relevant as contingency planning even if stated for information. Contingency plan, postponement/escalation decision record. Clinician-specific availability issues. Confirm what should be board assurance versus match operations procedure. A7: Medical duties Mandatory in substance Relevant to lead clinician accountability. Duty checklist, equipment sign-off, post-match confirmation. Individual clinical notes. Clarify whether duties sit with club doctor, lead physiotherapist, or contractor in mixed models. A8: Treatment in France Information only Relevant only when travelling to France/Toulouse or similar cross-border fixtures. Travel medical plan, insurance/indemnity check, medicine/travel records. Individual health records. Confirm when this applies to Championship clubs. B1: Medical note keeping and injury audit Mandatory Relevant to all club medical care. Record-system policy, access control, record completeness audit, injury audit evidence. Full clinical records. Confirm retention, audit sample, and RFL submission evidence. B2: Sharing information Mandatory in substance Relevant to players, coaches, contractors, parent clubs, and league reporting. Consent/confidentiality policy, disclosure log, role-access matrix. Detailed disclosure content and clinical reports. Align with GMC confidentiality and club data protection model. B3: Loan-player information sharing Mandatory Relevant where loan players are used. Loan handover checklist, notification record, restriction status. Detailed medical history. Confirm parent-club and loan-club minimum data set. B4: Death or serious injury reporting Mandatory Relevant to all matches/training. Serious incident chronology, notifications, board escalation, learning review. Clinical records and identifiable incident detail. Align RFL, insurer, safeguarding, and possible CQC notification routes. B5: Medical emergency action plan Mandatory Relevant for stadium, training, temporary venues, and venue moves. Site-specific EAP, ambulance access, ED details, review date, sharing log. Personal contact details where sensitive. Confirm temporary venue evidence standard. B6: Pre-match briefing Mandatory for home club Relevant to every home fixture. Briefing record, attendee list, emergency roles, opposition acknowledgement. None usually, unless player-specific risk is discussed. Define standard briefing evidence. B7: On-field processes Mixed Relevant where green card, blood bin, kick-off, or 18th-player rules apply. Fixture process checklist and exception log. Individual incident clinical record. Confirm which subsections apply to Championship and which are Super League-only. C1: Mandatory medical equipment and drugs box Mandatory Relevant to matches/training and travel. Inventory, expiry checks, oxygen/AED checks, missing-item escalation. Controlled medicine administration records. Confirm home/away and training responsibilities. C2: Equipment list Mixed mandatory and best practice Relevant to minimum kit and best-practice enhancements. Minimum mandatory equipment checklist, best-practice gap log. None usually. Separate mandatory kit from best-practice kit for Championship. C3: Clinical waste disposal Mandatory Relevant to treatment room and match/training care. Clinical waste contract/process, sharps checks, disposal audit. Incident records if exposure occurs. Confirm venue/contractor split. C4: Treatment-room facility standards Mandatory Relevant to training ground and stadium medical room. Room audit, access, handwashing, cleanable surfaces, privacy, internet, waste. None usually. Confirm temporary venue requirements. D1-D5: Anti-doping and medication checks Mixed mandatory and information-only Relevant to all player medicines and supplements. Policy, education records, medicine-check audit, TUE tracker status. Indication, diagnosis, prescription details, TUE medical evidence. Separate mandatory checks from special-topic guidance. E1-E8: Concussion and head injuries Mandatory regulations, with some competition-specific mechanisms Highly relevant to Championship. Baseline completion dashboard, HIA/RTP compliance status, reporting deadlines, exception actions. HIA form, SCAT6/Cognigram, clinical notes, specialist reports. Confirm Championship pitch-side replay, reporting deadline, and specialist referral requirements. F1-F2: Blood-borne infectious diseases Mandatory, with some best-practice elements Relevant to match/training bleeding injuries and infection control. BBV policy, bleeding injury process, hepatitis B screening/vaccination status at aggregate level. Individual screening/vaccination data. Confirm which screening/vaccination requirements apply to Championship. F3: Cardiac screening Competition-specific mandatory Relevant because RFL status includes Championship in the cardiac screening section. Screening plan, provider due diligence, completion dashboard, abnormal-result pathway assurance. Individual cardiac reports and referral records. Confirm provider registration/exemption model and exact player cohorts. F4: Turning players over Mandatory Relevant to all field-of-play care. Training record, spinal protocol, scenario rehearsal. Incident clinical details. Confirm training cadence. F5: Protective and other equipment Information only, with competition-specific equipment rules Relevant where mouth guards, head guards, boxes, padding, or instrumented mouth guards apply. Advice/education records and policy position. Individual medical exemptions. Confirm Championship-specific equipment obligations. F6-F8: Mental health counselling, training, insomnia Mixed information-only and mandatory training Relevant to player welfare and staff training. Welfare route, mental health training record, counselling access summary. Individual mental health records and referrals. Separate welfare, medical, HR, and safeguarding ownership. F9: Social and non-prescribed prescription drugs Mixed policy requirement and support pathway Relevant to player welfare, anti-doping, safeguarding, and discipline. Policy acknowledgement, case-review route, aggregate assurance. Individual case file. Confirm RFL escalation threshold and access restrictions. F10: Loan protocols Best practice Relevant where loan players move between clubs. Loan handover checklist and parent-club communication. Detailed clinical record. Decide whether to treat as local mandatory control despite best-practice RFL status. F11: Infection control Needs validation Relevant to treatment room, match-day care, and outbreaks. Infection-control policy, audit, cleaning record, action plan. Individual exposure/infection records. Confirm status wording and relationship to C3/F1-F2.

docs.title: Championship-specific research notes
docs.text:
• Cardiac screening is identified in the RFL standards as mandatory for specified competitions including Championship, but provider/control evidence still needs a CQC diagnostic/screening review.
  • Mandatory medical equipment and drugs box requirements apply across club contexts; exact home, away, training, academy, reserves, and scholarship responsibilities need extracting into a club-specific checklist.
  • Championship match-day cover requirements appear in competition tables; these should be converted into a short Championship cover register before any implementation pack is finalised.
  • Some controls are RFL best practice but may still be locally necessary because they reduce CQC-style governance risk.

docs.title: Source
docs.text:
This page maps the RFL Medical Standards 2026 to CQC-style assurance. The standards are part of the RFL Operational Rules and are mandatory where marked as mandatory.

For an operational clause-by-clause evidence checklist, use RFL Clause Evidence Map. This page remains the higher-level board and governance summary.

The 2026 standards expressly say they should be applied in accordance with UK healthcare legislation, professional regulator guidance, recognised professional bodies, best practice, and clinical governance standards such as CQC. That makes a CQC-style governance map appropriate even where the club is outside CQC registration. For the CQC assessment lens, see CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements.

docs.title: RFL structure and CQC alignment
docs.text: RFL Medical Standards section Core requirement CQC-style assurance domain Section A: Medical personnel Registration with the RFL, role definitions, prehospital trauma training, attendance at matches/training, match-day responsibilities. Safe; Effective; Well-led. Section B: Ethics, governance, planning, information and data Medical note keeping, sharing information, serious injury/death reporting, emergency action plans, pre-match briefing, on-field processes. Safe; Responsive; Well-led. Section C: Equipment and facilities Mandatory medical equipment, mandatory drugs box, clinical waste, treatment-room standards. Safe; Effective. Section D: Anti-doping Medication and supplement checking, prohibited substances, TUEs, IV infusions, special topics. Safe; Effective; Well-led. Section E: Concussion and head injuries Baselines, recognise and remove, HIA, post-match/training management, GRTP, reporting, SCOAT. Safe; Effective; Caring; Well-led. Section F: RFL medical policies Blood-borne diseases, cardiac screening, turning players, mental health, social and non-prescribed prescription drugs, infection control. Safe; Effective; Caring; Responsive. Appendix 1: IMMOFP Course eligibility, recertification, assessment, field-of-play competence. Safe and effective staffing. Appendix 2: Education CPD and anti-doping education expectations. Effective; Well-led.

docs.title: Medical personnel
docs.text:
The RFL's medical management page explains the IMMOFP/iIMMOFP training route and professional-game medical expectations. Club board assurance should evidence:

  • every clinician is registered with the RFL where required;
  • doctors, physiotherapists, paramedics, sports rehabilitators, and sports therapists operate within scope of practice;
  • IMMOFP or accepted equivalent qualifications are current;
  • locums and second medics receive induction and reporting instructions;
  • staff are protected from inappropriate pressure over medical decisions;
  • match-day and training cover is planned before fixtures are accepted.

docs.title: Medical records and information
docs.text:
The 2026 standards require clear, contemporaneous medical records, preferably electronic, encrypted, and secure. Records must be kept for any player receiving medical care or advice, including ad-hoc match cover.

Governance evidence:

  • named clinical record system;
  • access-control matrix;
  • retention schedule;
  • locum handover process;
  • consent and confidentiality policy;
  • loan-player information-sharing protocol;
  • audit of record completeness.

docs.title: Emergency action planning
docs.text:
Every club must have written medical emergency action plans for matches and training, covering grounds and training facilities used by the club.

Evidence:

  • site-specific emergency action plans;
  • ambulance access details and venue postcode/access points;
  • communication method with emergency services;
  • treatment-room and emergency department details;
  • next-of-kin notification arrangements;
  • arrangements for concussed players not driving home;
  • documented sharing with opposition medical staff;
  • pre-match briefing record.

docs.title: Equipment and facilities
docs.text:
RFL requires mandatory medical equipment and a mandatory drugs box at games and training, with home and away club obligations. Treatment rooms must meet defined standards including access for stretchers, handwashing, cleanable surfaces, sharps/clinical waste, and internet access for note keeping.

Evidence:

  • equipment inventory and expiry checks;
  • oxygen and AED checks;
  • mandatory drugs box expiry and stock checks;
  • equipment failure log;
  • treatment-room checklist;
  • clinical waste disposal contract/process;
  • infection-prevention audit.

docs.title: Concussion
docs.text:
The RFL standards and concussion guidance require annual baseline SCAT6 and Cognigram before contact activity, recognise-and-remove controls, HIA processes, GRTP, reporting within defined timescales, return-to-play forms, and specialist review in defined circumstances.

Evidence:

  • valid baseline register before contact training;
  • HIA/concussion reports within 24 hours;
  • parent-club notification within 12 hours where only the home doctor is present or the player is on loan;
  • GRTP oversight by a doctor;
  • doctor confirmation before progression to higher-risk stages;
  • return-to-play form submitted before match participation;
  • post-injury Cognigram file;
  • specialist reports and player/parent acknowledgment where required;
  • audit of missed, late, or non-compliant concussion steps.

docs.title: Cardiac screening
docs.text:
The 2026 standards make cardiac screening mandatory for Super League, Women's Super League, and Championship clubs. This creates both clinical governance and potential diagnostics/provider-control questions.

Evidence:

  • annual screening plan;
  • provider contract and registration/exemption analysis;
  • attendance register;
  • abnormal result escalation pathway;
  • player communication and records;
  • transfer/loan communication where relevant;
  • board-level assurance that screening is complete before relevant participation.

docs.title: CQC-style board dashboard from RFL standards
docs.text: Dashboard item Evidence source Medical staff compliance RFL registration, professional registration, IMMOFP status, indemnity, DBS where relevant, CPD. Match and training cover Fixture cover rota, EAPs, pre-match briefings, locum induction. Records Record audit, loan-player handovers, encryption/access checks. Serious incidents Death/serious injury notifications, emergency transfers, incident reviews. Equipment and facilities Mandatory medical equipment checks, drugs box checks, treatment-room audit. Concussion Baseline completion, HIA reports, GRTP compliance, RTP forms, specialist referrals. Medicines and anti-doping Global DRO checks, TUE status, supplement risk controls, emergency medicine governance. Infection and blood-borne disease BBV protocols, clinical waste, infection control audit. Mental health mandatory mental health training, counselling access, welfare escalation.

docs.title: Gap from RFL rules to CQC evidence
docs.text:
The RFL standards are strong sport-specific controls. The CQC-style additions are:

  • explicit legal entity accountability;
  • provider/control assessment for each healthcare service;
  • statement of purpose discipline;
  • complaints and duty-of-candour process where regulated care is provided;
  • medicines governance beyond match-day minimum kit, covered in Medicines, Prescribing, and Anti-Doping;
  • contractor quality assurance;
  • board risk register and formal closure of audit actions;
  • equality and player-experience evidence;
  • Freedom to Speak Up route for clinicians and players.

docs.title: Starting point
docs.text:
Professional Rugby League medical services are sophisticated healthcare environments, even when delivered for a closed athlete population. The Rugby Football League (RFL) medical management guidance describes professional game medical provision as including at least one Immediate Medical Management on the Field of Play (IMMOFP) qualified doctor at professional games, with physiotherapists, sports rehabilitators, and therapists also undergoing field-of-play training.

The governance challenge is that sports regulation, employment obligations, athlete welfare, and healthcare regulation overlap but do not fully substitute for each other.

docs.title: CQC sports exclusions
docs.text:
CQC's scope guidance for Treatment of disease, disorder or injury expressly excludes:

  • first aid delivered by healthcare professionals in unexpected or potentially dangerous situations requiring immediate action;
  • first aid by trained non-healthcare professionals;
  • first aid by organisations established for first-aid provision;
  • treatment provided in a sports ground or gymnasium, including associated premises, where it is solely for people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events;
  • treatment provided through temporary arrangements for sporting or cultural events.

That exclusion is central. A professional Rugby League club is less likely to need CQC registration merely because it provides pitch-side emergency care, match-day doctor cover, or training-ground treatment solely for players and attendees within the sports-ground/event context. The edge cases around public clinics, diagnostics, remote advice and contractor control are set out in CQC Registration Edge Cases.

docs.title: Professional sport is not automatically outside the exclusion
docs.text:
The CQC wording does not distinguish amateur sport from professional sport. It refers to treatment in a sports ground or gymnasium, associated premises, people taking part in or attending sporting activities and events, and temporary sporting-event arrangements. On that wording, the exclusion can apply to professional Rugby League where the facts meet those conditions.

For a Super League or Championship club, the professional nature of the service increases the need for evidence, but does not by itself remove the exclusion. The board should document:

  • the relevant location or temporary event;
  • the user group, limited to players, participants, match officials, staff necessary to the event, or attendees as relevant;
  • that the care is not advertised or operated as a public/private clinic;
  • that diagnostics, remote triage, ambulance transport to hospital, public treatment, or paid non-player services have been separately assessed;
  • which legal entity controls the service and any contractor.

The conclusion should be phrased carefully: professional pitch-side/player care may be excluded from CQC registration for Treatment of disease, disorder or injury; the club's wider medical service model may still create CQC registration duties.

docs.title: Where CQC risk can re-enter
docs.text:
The sports exclusion is not a blanket immunity for all healthcare run by a club. CQC's scope of registration should be reassessed if the club does any of the following:

  • runs a clinic for members of the public, staff, sponsors, academy parents, schools, or other third parties;
  • charges for physiotherapy, sports medicine, diagnostics, injections, screening, or rehabilitation outside the club's participant/attendee population;
  • operates treatment from premises that function as a private clinic rather than a sports ground, gymnasium, or associated sports premises;
  • provides remote triage, medical advice, or treatment plans as a service beyond match/training participation;
  • carries out diagnostic and screening procedures as a sole or main activity;
  • arranges imaging, blood tests, injections, prescribing, or minor procedures under the club's own clinical governance rather than merely referring to an external registered provider;
  • contracts clinicians but retains direction and control over regulated healthcare activities;
  • provides services to children or academy players in a way that raises both healthcare and safeguarding governance obligations.

docs.title: Rugby League governance already in place
docs.text:
The RFL Medical Standards 2026 create useful governance inputs:

  • IMMOFP and iIMMOFP training routes;
  • professional registration expectations for doctors, physiotherapists, paramedics, sports therapists, and sports rehabilitators;
  • indemnity expectations for working in sport;
  • concussion rules, return-to-play protocols, and reporting, supported by the RFL's concussion guidance;
  • clinical groups and CPD activity;
  • first-aider registration and match-day monitoring in the community game.

These are strong sport-specific controls. The CQC-style gap is that they do not, by themselves, prove that the club has a full healthcare provider governance system covering legal entity accountability, regulated activity scope, clinical records, complaints, duty of candour, audit, risk management, data protection, premises, equipment, medicines, and provider-level board assurance. The RFL Medical Standards Map converts those controls into CQC-style evidence.

docs.title: Applied governance map
docs.text: Club medical activity Likely CQC posture Governance control Pitch-side emergency treatment during fixtures Usually within first-aid or temporary sporting-event exclusion Event medical plan, IMMOFP rota, equipment checks, incident log, escalation to ambulance/ED. Training-ground player treatment in club medical room Likely sports-ground/gym exclusion if solely for participants and associated premises Medical-room SOPs, records, consent, safeguarding, concussion protocol, medicines control. Rehabilitation for contracted players only Often sports participant context, but check premises and clinical control Rehab governance, clinical supervision, scope of practice, outcomes and re-injury audit. Private sports clinic open to public Likely CQC assessment needed if regulated activity is provided Treat as independent healthcare provider; register if no exemption applies. Diagnostic ultrasound, imaging, blood testing, cardiac screening Potential diagnostic/screening regulated activity depending on model Decide who is provider; use CQC-registered diagnostic provider or register. Club doctor prescribing/injections for players May be covered if within sports-ground participant treatment, but high governance risk Prescribing policy, PGDs where relevant, medicines storage, consent, adverse incident review. Academy medical care for children Sports exclusion may still matter, but safeguarding bar is higher Safeguarding lead, parental consent, chaperones, DBS, child records, escalation routes. Contracted event medical company Provider may be contractor, club, or both depending on control Contract must state clinical control, registration status, standards, records, incidents, indemnity.

docs.title: Decision diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TD
  A["Medical activity proposed by club"] --> B{"Is it health care for disease, disorder, injury, diagnostics, screening, surgery, transport, or triage?"}
  B -- "No" --> C["CQC registration unlikely; manage through sport, H&S, safeguarding, and employment governance"]
  B -- "Yes" --> D{"Is it solely for players/participants/attendees at sports ground, gym, associated premises, or temporary sporting event?"}
  D -- "Yes" --> E["Likely sports/first-aid exclusion; document rationale and keep CQC-style governance controls"]
  D -- "No or unclear" --> F{"Who directs and controls the care?"}
  F --> G["Club"]
  F --> H["Independent provider"]
  F --> I["Shared or unclear"]
  G --> J["Club should assess CQC registration and regulated activities"]
  H --> K["Verify provider registration/exemption, contract, records, incident duties, and indemnity"]
  I --> L["Resolve control in contract; multiple entities may need registration analysis"]

docs.title: Practical conclusion
docs.text:
For a professional Rugby League club, the prudent position is not "CQC never applies to sport." It is:

  1. Core pitch-side and participant-only care may be excluded from CQC registration.
  2. Wider private healthcare, diagnostics, and clinic-style services can fall back into CQC scope.
  3. Even where excluded, CQC governance standards are a useful benchmark for quality, safety, and board assurance.

docs.title: Scope Decision Record
docs.text: This page records the current scope decision for the research. It should be revisited when the intended output changes.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
The working scope is an online CQC inspection preparation platform for professional sports clubs, using a Championship Rugby League Club as the starting example.

The platform should help the organisation:

  • appreciate what CQC is and why it may matter;
  • understand how a CQC-style assessment or inspection would work;
  • identify whether its medical service is outside scope, excluded, contractor-led, or potentially registerable;
  • understand what evidence it would need to prepare;
  • move from existing medical/clinical governance to CQC inspection-level readiness;
  • do this in a context where CQC preparation is new to the organisation.

This does not mean the club is assumed to require CQC registration. The research remains focused on understanding, preparation, and evidence.

docs.title: Audience
docs.text:
The audience is not yet a commercial buying group. The primary audience is a professional sports club that needs to learn and prepare for CQC inspection-level scrutiny, with a Championship Rugby League Club used as the first worked example.

Audience Need Board/CEO Understand risk, accountability, inspection exposure, decisions needed, and what assurance should look like. Head of Medical Translate existing clinical governance into CQC-style evidence, gaps, and preparation actions. Clinical team Understand records, professional boundaries, incidents, medicines, concussion, confidentiality, and evidence expectations. Operations/match-day staff Understand EAP, venue, treatment-room, equipment, contractor, ambulance, and communication evidence. Safeguarding/welfare staff Understand where clinical governance overlaps with welfare, academy, mental health, and restricted information. External providers Understand what evidence the club needs from cardiac screening, event medical, diagnostics, or other contractors.

docs.title: Immediate output
docs.text:
The immediate output is an online preparation and assurance knowledge base, not a software implementation specification.

It should be able to support:

  • a board-facing Head of Medical briefing;
  • a CQC scope decision record;
  • a minimum compliance/evidence pack;
  • RFL standards evidence mapping;
  • practical worked examples;
  • a gap analysis against inspection-style expectations;
  • a preparation route for a club that has never approached CQC before.

docs.title: Rejected or secondary scopes
docs.text: Scope Current treatment Full SaaS product specification Deferred. Too early until research and validation are clearer. Commercial buyer/persona modelling Deferred. Audience is organisational learning and preparation, not procurement. All professional sports in depth Secondary. The platform scope can be broader than Rugby League, but the first research examples should stay concrete. All Rugby League competitions Secondary. Championship is the starting example; Super League, WSL, academy, reserves, and community are comparison points. Legal advice product Out of scope. The wiki can identify advice triggers but not provide legal determinations. Clinical decision-support tool Out of scope. Clinical safety and medical-device issues would need separate analysis.

docs.title: What this means for roadmap items
docs.text: Item Scope implication 1. Research audience and decision context Now provisionally decided: online CQC inspection preparation platform for professional sports clubs, starting with a Championship Rugby League Club new to CQC. 2. Minimum compliance pack Should focus on what a club needs to understand and gather before inspection-style scrutiny, not on final product forms. 3. RFL clause map Should prioritise Championship relevance and board/Head of Medical interpretation. 4. Platform information architecture Remains deferred, but future work can consider knowledge-base structure and preparation journey before software data models. 5. CQC scope question bank Should be phrased as guided preparation and fact gathering, not an automated registration answer. 6. Tool landscape Should remain a comparator for what existing tools can teach or support, not a vendor selection exercise.

docs.title: Assumptions to test
docs.text:
• A professional sports club may have medical and clinical governance, but not in a form that is obviously ready for CQC-style inspection.
  • A board and Head of Medical need plain-English preparation material before operational tooling.
  • The most useful first output is likely a guided evidence and understanding pathway, not a transactional compliance system.
  • The club needs to separate board assurance from confidential clinical records.
  • Existing tools may support parts of the evidence burden, but the club first needs to know what evidence matters.

docs.title: Open decisions
docs.text: Open decision Why it matters Is Sheffield Eagles the named working example, or should the site remain generic Championship? Determines tone, examples, venue specificity, and board-pack realism. Should academy/scholarship be included in first-phase scope? Adds safeguarding, parental consent, and child welfare complexity. Should the online platform be public education, internal club preparation, or both? Changes language, confidentiality, and evidence examples. Should the next output be a guided preparation pathway through the wiki? Would change navigation from research sections to task-based preparation. Should the platform compare "current governance" against "CQC inspection-grade governance"? Could be the clearest bridge for a club new to CQC.

docs.title: Next research direction
docs.text:
The next useful research direction is to define the preparation journey:

  1. What a professional sports club needs to understand first.
  2. What facts it must collect.
  3. What evidence it must hold.
  4. What gaps it should expect.
  5. What should be escalated to legal, CQC, league, governing-body, or vendor review.

That journey should be written for a professional sports club that has not previously prepared for CQC and may not know what "inspection-grade" evidence looks like.

docs.title: Tool Capability Comparison
docs.text:
This page hardens item 6 by comparing relevant tool categories against the evidence needs now emerging from the wiki. It should be read with Platform Tool Landscape and Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix.

This remains research. It does not select a vendor or define a build decision.

docs.title: Capability comparison
docs.text: Capability Healthcare/CQC operations tools Sports EMR/AMS tools Sports governance/safeguarding tools Research implication CQC policy and inspection readiness Strong in tools such as Agilio TeamNet, Radar, QCS, and Vantage. Usually not CQC-specific. Usually not CQC-specific. Useful comparator for evidence packs and audit discipline. Significant events, incidents, and action tracking Strong in healthcare compliance tools; variable by vendor. Present where sports injury/medical incidents are captured. Strong for safeguarding/welfare incidents. Need to separate clinical incidents, safeguarding, RFL reporting, and CQC-style governance. Workforce training and acknowledgements Strong in Agilio-style healthcare operations and some compliance tools. Variable. Strong where vetting/certification is central. Useful for RFL medical staff, CPD, anti-doping, mental health training, and policy acknowledgement. Athlete clinical records Generally weak or generic. Strong in Kitman Labs, Teamworks AMS, AthleteMonitoring, SportsWareOnLine, and Orreco-style systems. Usually weak. A CQC/RFL evidence layer should not assume it can replace sports EMR without research. Concussion and return-to-play Generic in CQC tools; stronger in sport-specific platforms. Strong in mature sports EMRs, especially where SCAT/SCOAT/baselines are supported. Sometimes present as eligibility/compliance workflow. Need to test whether exports satisfy RFL and board assurance needs. Medicines, stock, TUE, anti-doping Present in some healthcare and sports EMR systems, but not always anti-doping-specific. Often stronger for athlete context. Usually limited. Must verify Global DRO/TUE evidence, restricted access, and audit trail. Contractor due diligence Strong pattern in healthcare compliance tools. Usually not a core feature. Variable. Important for cardiac screening, ambulance/event cover, diagnostics, and external clinicians. CQC scope decision evidence General CQC context may exist. Usually absent. Usually absent. This remains the most distinctive research gap. Board assurance without clinical detail Strong dashboard pattern in compliance tools; sports EMRs may expose availability summaries. Strong for performance/availability, weaker for CQC governance. Strong for safeguarding/compliance status. Need to validate what board should see and what must remain restricted. Data export/API Vendor-specific. Mature vendors often advertise export/API capabilities. Variable. Procurement must test export quality, not just feature claims.

docs.title: Vendor verification questions
docs.text: Vendor/category Questions to ask before relying on it Agilio TeamNet / healthcare operations tools Can policies, significant events, staff training, review dates, safety alerts, and audit trails be configured around RFL standards? Can it separate confidential player clinical data from board assurance? Can it export evidence packs? Radar / healthcare risk and audit tools Can incidents, audits, risks, and actions be mapped to RFL clauses and CQC evidence categories? Can contractor due diligence and match-day evidence be handled without over-configuration? QCS / policy and compliance content tools Is the value mainly policy content, or can evidence, actions, and board assurance be operationalised? Can sports-specific documents be maintained cleanly? Vantage / compliance and quality management Can quality management workflows support sports medical edge cases, contractors, and RFL evidence? What export and permission model is available? Kitman Labs / sports EMR Can concussion, medicines, TUE, screening, injury, and clinical records be exported into governance evidence without exposing unnecessary clinical detail? Teamworks AMS / Smartabase Can forms, dashboards, athlete records, and exports support RFL/CQC evidence mapping? What API/export options exist? AthleteMonitoring / SportsWareOnLine / Orreco Which clinical records, SOAP notes, medicine, injury, and return-to-play fields are exportable, and how is confidentiality controlled? Sport:80 / SportLoMo / CPOMS / Globocol Can safeguarding, vetting, eligibility, incident, and compliance workflows integrate with medical governance without conflating safeguarding records and clinical notes?

docs.title: Research conclusions
docs.text:
• Healthcare operations tools look strongest for policies, training, significant events, review dates, audit trails, and inspection-readiness patterns.
  • Sports EMR/AMS tools look strongest for day-to-day athlete medical records, concussion, injury lifecycle, medication, screening, and return-to-play evidence.
  • Sports governance/safeguarding tools look strongest for eligibility, vetting, safeguarding, welfare, policy acknowledgement, and federation-style reporting.
  • No researched category has yet been shown to cover CQC scope reasoning, RFL clause evidence, board-safe assurance, contractor provider-control analysis, and sports clinical records in one package.
  • The next research should test exports and permissions against the Minimum Compliance Pack Scenario Matrix, not just compare marketing claims.

docs.title: Wiki Visual Tooling Register
docs.text: This page records visual and knowledge-map tools that could be added when the research needs them. The intent is not to install every tool immediately. The intent is to keep a practical register so new information can be matched to the right visual form.

docs.title: Current baseline
docs.text:
The wiki currently uses Material for MkDocs with PyMdown SuperFences configured for Mermaid diagrams.

Mermaid is already suitable for:

  • decision trees;
  • governance flowcharts;
  • sequence diagrams;
  • state diagrams;
  • entity-relationship diagrams;
  • simple journey maps.

It should remain the default for diagrams that are small enough to read directly in Markdown and where source control readability matters.

docs.title: Tool register
docs.text: Tool What it adds Use when Current posture Mermaid in Material for MkDocs Text-based flowcharts, sequence diagrams, ER diagrams, state diagrams, and related diagram types. A process or decision can be expressed clearly as code in a Markdown fence. Enabled. Default first choice. mkdocs-drawio Embeds .drawio / diagrams.net files as normal Markdown images. Supports multi-page diagrams, hosted diagrams, print/edit buttons, dark mode, and Material compatibility. A board-facing or operational diagram is too visual or layout-heavy for Mermaid. Strong candidate to enable first. Static SVG, PNG, or WebP assets Stable image assets copied through the MkDocs static build. A diagram is final, brand/presentation-focused, or should not depend on runtime JavaScript. Already available through ordinary Markdown image links. mkdocs-glightbox Click-to-zoom image lightbox for diagrams and screenshots. Diagrams, screenshots, or evidence maps are too wide to read inline. Strong candidate alongside draw.io. mkdocs-caption Figure and table captions, numbering, and cross-reference text. The wiki needs report-style figures, board pack references, or formal numbered exhibits. Candidate once visuals become board/report outputs. mkdocs-charts-plugin / Vega-Lite Declarative charts from JSON/data using Vega-Lite. We have structured evidence, maturity, risk, validation, or inspection-readiness data to visualize. Candidate after data structure stabilises. mkdocs-plotly-plugin Interactive charts from Plotly JSON. We need richer interactive charts than Vega-Lite, such as drillable dashboards or complex hover detail. Later candidate; heavier than Vega-Lite. mkdocs-table-reader-plugin Inserts tables from CSV, JSON, Excel, YAML, TSV, and related files. Evidence maps, RFL clause registers, vendor comparisons, or maturity tables should become data files rather than hand-written Markdown tables. Candidate when tables become large or repeated. mkdocs-markmap / Markmap Interactive mind maps from Markdown hierarchy. A research topic needs a browsable concept map rather than a strict process diagram. Candidate for research scoping and topic maps. mkdocs-kroki-plugin / Kroki One route to many diagram languages, including PlantUML, C4, GraphViz, D2, BPMN, Excalidraw, Vega, Vega-Lite, WaveDrom, and more. Mermaid and draw.io are not enough, or a specialist notation is needed. Powerful but heavier; consider privacy and hosting before enabling. mkdocs-network-graph-plugin Interactive knowledge network graph for Material for MkDocs, with full-site and local page connections. The wiki needs an Obsidian-like site map showing how research pages connect. Best MkDocs-native knowledge-map candidate. mkdocs-obsidian-interactive-graph-plugin Obsidian-like interactive graph for Material for MkDocs using ECharts and wikilinks. The wiki moves toward Obsidian-style wikilinks and wants a closer Obsidian visual pattern. Secondary knowledge-map candidate; more moving parts than network graph. mkdocstrings/autorefs Cross-page references, anchors, and backlink recording for other plugins or hooks. Backlinks, referenced-by sections, or richer cross-reference behavior become important. Infrastructure candidate, not a visual graph by itself. Foam graph visualization VS Code knowledge graph for Markdown notes, including files, tags, links, filters, and hover/click navigation. Editors want a local authoring graph while writing the wiki. Authoring-side option, not a published MkDocs site feature.

docs.title: Obsidian-like knowledge map finding
docs.text:
Yes. The closest published-wiki options are:

  1. mkdocs-network-graph-plugin: best fit for this site if we want a public, Material-compatible knowledge graph. It is described as an interactive knowledge network graph for Material for MkDocs and supports both full-site overview and local page connections.
  2. mkdocs-obsidian-interactive-graph-plugin: closest in intent to Obsidian, but it currently relies on wikilinks, JavaScript assets, jQuery, and ECharts. It may be useful if the wiki adopts Obsidian-style [[wikilinks]], but it is a less conservative first choice.
  3. Foam graph visualization: useful for local authoring in VS Code, but not a native published-site graph.
  4. autorefs backlinks: useful if the site needs backlink sections or graph data, but it does not itself render an Obsidian-style graph.

The recommended first experiment is mkdocs-network-graph-plugin on a branch, because it is designed for Material for MkDocs, has simple plugins: - graph setup, and avoids changing the wiki writing style to wikilinks.

docs.title: Use triggers
docs.text: New information type Prefer CQC scope decision pathway Mermaid first; draw.io if board-facing. Board assurance journey draw.io, then static SVG export if stable. Evidence pack structure Mermaid or markmap. RFL clause maturity dashboard Vega-Lite or Plotly once data-backed. Large RFL/vendor/evidence tables table-reader from CSV or YAML. Knowledge relationships across pages mkdocs-network-graph-plugin. Research concept map markmap. Formal board/report exhibit caption plus glightbox. Architecture/product modules Mermaid, draw.io, or Kroki C4/PlantUML. BPMN-style workflow Kroki BPMN or draw.io. Hand-drawn workshop sketch style Kroki Excalidraw or draw.io.

docs.title: Adoption order
docs.text:
1. Shortlist now: Mermaid, draw.io, static SVG/PNG, glightbox, caption, markmap, table-reader, Vega-Lite, Plotly, Kroki, network graph, Obsidian interactive graph, autorefs, Foam.
  2. Install only when needed: do not add plugins without an immediate page or research output that will use them.
  3. First likely install: mkdocs-drawio and mkdocs-glightbox, because they directly improve board-facing diagrams and large evidence maps.
  4. First knowledge-map experiment: mkdocs-network-graph-plugin on a branch.
  5. Data-visualisation install: add table-reader and Vega-Lite/charts only after evidence registers are converted to structured data.
  6. Kroki decision: use only if we need specialist diagram languages and have decided whether to use hosted Kroki or a self-hosted service.

docs.title: Guardrails
docs.text:
• Prefer text-based diagrams where the source should be reviewable in Git.
  • Prefer draw.io for diagrams that need manual layout, board polish, or iterative discussion.
  • Prefer static SVG exports for visuals that should remain stable in LLM exports, printed material, or board packs.
  • Avoid adding JavaScript-heavy interactive visuals unless they answer a real research question.
  • Check whether a plugin changes Markdown syntax, page loading, search, or GitHub Pages compatibility before enabling it.
  • Check privacy and dependency implications before using hosted rendering services such as Kroki.
  • Keep LLM wiki usability in mind: every important visual should also have a short textual explanation nearby.

docs.title: Worked Examples
docs.text:
These examples show how to apply the Registration Decision Model, CQC Registration Edge Cases, and Assurance Pack Templates to realistic Rugby League club medical services.

The examples are not legal determinations. They identify the likely posture, the reason, and the evidence a club should hold before relying on an exclusion or launching a new service.

docs.title: Summary matrix
docs.text: Example Likely CQC posture Main risk Primary control Player-only training room Usually lower risk if solely participant care at associated sports premises Service creep into staff/public treatment Statement of purpose and eligibility boundary. Public sports injury clinic High registration risk Club becomes independent healthcare provider CQC scope assessment before launch. Cardiac screening by external provider Medium risk Diagnostic provider/control ambiguity Contract, registration/exemption check, results governance. Away fixture concussion Lower CQC registration risk, high governance risk Records, HIA reporting, handover, GRTP control RFL concussion process and clinical record audit. Club doctor injection or prescribing Medium to high risk Medicines, anti-doping, registration boundary Medicines log, Global DRO/TUE check, clinical indication. Remote injury triage app Medium to high risk Formal remote triage/advice service CQC remote advice assessment and safety-netting. Contractor event medical cover Medium risk Unclear provider/control and incident duties Contractor due diligence and quality reporting. Academy medical pathway Medium governance risk Children, consent, safeguarding, confidentiality Safeguarding, consent, records, parent communication.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The club operates a treatment room at its training ground. Club-employed or contracted clinicians assess and treat contracted players, academy players, and trialists for Rugby League injuries. The room is not advertised to the public and does not charge separate treatment fees.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: This is usually lower CQC registration risk if the activity is solely for people taking part in sporting activity and is delivered at a sports ground, gymnasium, or associated premises. The relevant external source is CQC's Treatment of disease, disorder or injury guidance, which includes sport and first-aid exclusions. The same analysis can apply in professional sport; the professional status of the club does not, by itself, defeat the exclusion.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: The activity looks like participant-only sports medical care, not a public healthcare service. However, the club still controls clinicians, records, premises, medicines, referrals, and return-to-play decisions, so it needs a CQC-style governance system even if registration is not required.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• A statement of purpose saying the room is for players/participants only.
  • Clinical records and information-governance controls.
  • Treatment-room and equipment checks aligned to the RFL Medical Standards 2026.
  • Concussion, safeguarding, medicines, infection-control, chaperone, and emergency action policies.
  • Annual review showing the room has not drifted into public or paid private care.

docs.title: Do before change
docs.text: If the club wants to treat staff families, sponsors, local schools, community players, or the public, rerun the CQC scope assessment.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The club opens the medical room two evenings per week for paying members of the public with sports injuries. Appointments are promoted on the club website and delivered by the club's doctor and physiotherapists.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: High CQC registration risk. This looks much closer to independent healthcare than participant-only sports treatment. Start with CQC's scope of registration, independent healthcare services, and Treatment of disease, disorder or injury guidance.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: The public clinic is not solely for people taking part in or attending a sporting event. The club may be carrying on treatment of disease, disorder or injury, especially where a doctor assesses, prescribes, injects, supervises, or directs treatment.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• Full regulated activity analysis.
  • Legal entity and provider/control analysis.
  • Statement of purpose.
  • Registered manager and nominated individual analysis if registration is needed.
  • Policies for complaints, duty of candour, safeguarding, consent, staffing, medicines, records, and premises.

docs.title: Do before launch
docs.text: Do not launch until the club has a documented registration decision and, if required, CQC registration. Use Private Healthcare Synergies to structure the provider model.

docs.title: Example 3: Cardiac screening by external provider
Scenario
docs.text: The club commissions an external cardiac screening company to run annual screening for first team, academy, and women's squads at the training ground.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: Medium CQC risk. The clinical service may involve diagnostic and screening procedures. The key question is who carries on the diagnostic activity and who controls reporting, onward referral, and clinical governance.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: The RFL Medical Standards 2026 make cardiac screening mandatory for some clubs, but RFL compliance does not settle the CQC provider question. If the external provider controls the clinical service and is appropriately registered or exempt, the club's role may be commissioning and governance. If the club controls the screening, reporting, or clinical pathway, the risk shifts back toward the club.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• External provider CQC registration or written exemption rationale.
  • Contract defining provider control, clinical responsibility, results reporting, abnormal-result escalation, records, and indemnity.
  • Attendance register and completion dashboard.
  • Abnormal result pathway and communication record.
  • Board assurance that the club has not accidentally become the diagnostic provider.

docs.title: Do before renewal
docs.text: Run the contractor due diligence checklist annually and whenever the provider changes.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: A player suffers a suspected concussion during an away fixture where only the home doctor is present. The player is removed, assessed, and returns home under the away club's care.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: Lower CQC registration risk in the match-event context, but high clinical governance risk. Use the RFL concussion guidance and the concussion section of the RFL Medical Standards 2026.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: The service likely sits inside the sporting-event or participant-care setting, even in a professional fixture, because CQC's sports/event wording is not limited to amateur sport. The risk is not usually public registration for the pitch-side assessment itself. It is handover, reporting, records, medication masking symptoms, return-to-play pressure, and missed specialist referral.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• HIA/concussion report within required RFL timescale.
  • Parent-club notification and handover record.
  • Player advice sheet and travel/driving advice.
  • Medication review.
  • GRTP log overseen by a doctor.
  • Post-injury Cognigram and return-to-play form where required.
  • Specialist review where RFL criteria are met.

docs.title: Do before next match
docs.text: Use the concussion assurance checklist before the player is eligible for match participation.

docs.title: Example 5: Club doctor injection or prescribing
Scenario
docs.text: A club doctor prescribes analgesia and administers an injection to a player to treat an acute Rugby League injury during the season.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: Medium to high governance risk. CQC registration depends on whether the treatment is within the sport/participant exclusion and who controls the activity, but medicines, consent, records, professional duties, and anti-doping controls apply regardless. Relevant sources include CQC's medicines optimisation, CQC's Treatment of disease, disorder or injury, UKAD Search Check Apply, and GMC prescribing guidance.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Prescribing and administering medicines are high-risk healthcare acts. In elite sport, the additional layer is anti-doping status, TUE timing, pressure to play, and whether medication could mask symptoms or worsen risk.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• Clinical indication and assessment.
  • Consent, risks, benefits, alternatives, and return-to-play implications.
  • Allergy, interaction, contraindication, and current medication check.
  • Global DRO result or UKAD advice.
  • TUE decision or note that none is required.
  • Dose, route, batch where relevant, clinician, time, follow-up.
  • Adverse event monitoring and record of player advice.

docs.title: Do before repeat use
docs.text: Review through the medicines lead and include in the medicines and anti-doping log. Patterned or repeated use should trigger audit.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The club offers a remote app where academy players, parents, and community partner teams can submit injury symptoms and receive same-day advice from club clinicians.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: Medium to high CQC risk. CQC's transport, triage and medical advice provided remotely guidance should be checked before launch.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Occasional remote follow-up for known club players is different from a formal remote advice or triage service. Risk increases if the body is established for triage, prioritises urgent needs, gives immediate advice, or serves people outside the club participant group.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• Scope assessment distinguishing follow-up from triage service.
  • Eligibility rules and age/parental consent model.
  • Clinical safety-netting scripts.
  • Escalation to GP, NHS 111, emergency department, ambulance, or safeguarding.
  • Record system and response-time monitoring.
  • Data protection and confidentiality review.

docs.title: Do before launch
docs.text: Run the CQC scope assessment and get specialist advice if the service is available beyond contracted club players.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The club contracts an event medical provider for home fixtures. The contractor supplies paramedics, ambulance capability, first aiders, and pitch-side support. Club doctors also attend.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: Medium risk. Match-day sports treatment may be excluded, but transport, ambulance movement to hospital, contractor registration, clinical control, incident reporting, and records must be clear. CQC's transport and remote triage guidance is relevant where ambulance transport is provided.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: The club may be the organiser, the contractor may be the clinical provider, or responsibilities may be shared. The professional status of the fixture does not decide CQC registration; the contractor's activities, locations, users, transport arrangements, and clinical control do. If the contract is vague, the club may not know who owns incidents, complaints, clinical records, safeguarding escalation, duty of candour, or CQC notifications if a regulated activity is carried on.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• Contractor legal entity, CQC registration or exemption analysis.
  • Named clinical lead and command structure.
  • Emergency action plan and pre-match briefing record.
  • Equipment, medicines, ambulance, and communication checks.
  • Incident, complaint, safeguarding, and serious injury escalation route.
  • Post-event quality report.

docs.title: Do before season start
docs.text: Complete the contractor due diligence checklist and rehearse command-and-control with club clinicians.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: The academy medical team assesses and treats under-18 players, shares information with parents, coaches, schools, and external specialists, and manages concussion return-to-learn and return-to-play.

docs.title: Likely posture
docs.text: Medium governance risk. CQC registration may still be lower where care is solely for sports participants at associated premises, but safeguarding, consent, confidentiality, and information sharing require stronger controls.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Under-18 care introduces child safeguarding, parental responsibility, capacity, confidentiality, chaperone, welfare, school, and family communication issues. The club also needs to manage pressure from selection, contracts, scholarships, and progression routes.

docs.title: Evidence to hold
docs.text:
• Safeguarding lead and escalation pathway.
  • Parent/guardian and player consent model.
  • Confidentiality policy explaining what is shared with coaches and parents.
  • Chaperone and intimate-examination process.
  • DBS/credential checks for relevant clinicians.
  • Return-to-learn and return-to-play communication.
  • Academy-specific incident and complaint route.

docs.title: Do before pre-season
docs.text: Include academy care in the annual medical governance review and the five-domain board dashboard.

docs.title: How to use these examples
docs.text:
For each new or changed service:

  1. Select the closest worked example.
  2. Complete the CQC scope assessment.
  3. Identify the relevant CQC domain and quality statements in CQC Assurance Domains and Quality Statements.
  4. Add the controls to the Governance Operating Model.
  5. Record the decision, evidence, owner, and next review date.

docs.text:

  • RFL Medical Standards Map maps sport-specific obligations to CQC-style assurance.
  • Medicines, Prescribing, and Anti-Doping covers the high-risk medicines examples.
  • Assurance Pack Templates provides the forms and checklists referenced above.
